design.area: execution graph + run lifecycle (registry.ts getNextStep/rewindRun, executor signal dispatch, StepState model, new ctx.rerun + engine.rerunStep verbs) — plus a host-side child-workflow-per-unit recommendation
design.summary: A durable `rerunStep(runId, stepId)` that re-executes one completed step and only its TRUE downstream dependents, leaving independent completed steps untouched. Today this is impossible because the graph is a pure array-index `+1` (registry.ts:42) and the only reset verb, `rewindRun` (registry.ts:89), forward-cascades EVERY step with `index >= targetIndex` — it has no dataflow notion. The honest recommendation is BOTH, split by altitude: (a) add an OPTIONAL explicit `deps: string[]` per step + a `getDependents()` closure walk + a new `rerunRun()` registry verb that invalidates only the transitive-dependent closure, shipped as an additive engine primitive; and (b) document the child-workflow-per-unit pattern (each shot/order-line a child run) as the PREFERRED host topology for fan-out units that must rerun in true isolation, which needs ZERO engine change and already works via startChildWorkflow (context.ts:163) + child-await (engine.ts:906-1005). The deps graph upgrades the engine's core capability from linear-with-jumps to a real DAG with selective invalidation, while keeping all durability invariants. Crucially, deps are STRUCTURE (which pre-registered handler feeds which), never business logic — so it stays inside the primitives mandate.
design.publicApi:
Additive, all optional:

1) Step-level deps (define.ts StepConfig + core/types.ts Step):
   interface StepConfig { ...; deps?: string[] }  // IDs of steps whose output this step consumes
   interface Step { ...; deps?: string[] }
   Threaded in toStepDef (define.ts:138). Absent deps => engine infers implicit linear dep on the prior step (back-compat: linear graph is preserved exactly).

2) Engine verb (define.ts WorkflowHandle / WorkflowEngine, exported via createWorkflow):
   engine.rerunStep(runId: string, stepId: string, opts?: {
     cascade?: 'dependents' | 'none';   // default 'dependents'
     reason?: string;
   }): Promise<WorkflowRun<TContext>>
   - 'dependents': resets `stepId` + its transitive dependents to 'pending', leaves all non-dependent done steps untouched, sets currentStepId=stepId, status='running', re-arms the run loop.
   - 'none': reruns ONLY stepId (host asserts no downstream consumes its output).
   Throws StepNotFoundError (unknown id), InvalidStateError (run terminal/cancelled).

3) In-handler control-flow primitive (StepContext core/types.ts:295 + context.ts), mirroring goto:
   ctx.rerunSelf(): Promise<never>   // throw RerunSignal(stepId) for self-retry-as-fresh (attempts reset, output cleared)
   (Optional; only if a step wants to re-arm itself idempotently. Lower priority than rerunStep.)

4) New error class (index.ts:315 group): none required — reuses StepNotFoundError + InvalidStateError.

No change to the linear default. Projects that never set `deps` see identical behavior.
design.internalChanges:
Real seams touched (all in D:/projects/packages/streamline/src):

- core/types.ts:4-93 — add optional `deps?: string[]` to Step. No new StepState field is strictly required for cascade, but add `lastRerunAt?: Date` + `rerunReason?: string` to StepState (types.ts:133-150) + StepStateSchema (run.model.ts:10-30) for observability/audit. These are additive Mixed/Date fields, no index needed.
- workflow/define.ts:138 toStepDef — copy entry.deps onto Step. Validate in validateWorkflowDefinition (utils/validation.ts) that every dep references a real step id AND appears earlier in the steps array (forward-only DAG; no cycles, no forward refs) — cheap topological guard at definition time.
- workflow/registry.ts — NEW methods alongside getNextStep/rewindRun:
    getDependents(stepId): Set<string>  // transitive closure over deps (reverse edges), built once per definition, memoized.
    rerunRun(run, targetStepId, cascade): WorkflowRun  // sibling to rewindRun (registry.ts:89). Instead of `index >= targetIndex`, it resets ONLY { targetStepId } ∪ getDependents(targetStepId). Each reset uses the SAME fresh-state shape as rewindRun:89-106 (clear output/error/startedAt/endedAt/waitingFor/retryAfter, status='pending', attempts=0). Sets currentStepId=targetStepId, status='running', clears run-level output/endedAt/error.
- execution/engine.ts:1428 — NEW rerunStep() method modeled on rewindTo():1428. It loads run (getOrThrow), guards terminal/cancelled, calls registry.rerunRun, then — CRITICAL — writes via NARROW per-step $set fields (the steps.<i>.* update map shape used in handleGoto executor.ts:766-787), NOT updateById(rewoundRun) like rewindTo does (rewindTo's full-doc updateById is the mongoose-9 subdoc-coercion hazard called out at engine.ts:1451-1463; rerunStep must avoid it). Then re-arm the run loop (same path rewindTo's caller uses / execute()).
- execution/engine.ts:846-865 checkNoProgress — rerunStep keeps currentStepId potentially equal to a prior value; it must signal progress. rerunRun marks the rerun target 'pending' from 'done', which is a state change checkNoProgress already tolerates (currentStep.status !== prevStepStatus). But add the same explicit escape hatch goto uses: if the target step transitioned done→pending, treat as progress. Verify against the no-progress detector or the run wedges (invariant #7).
- workflow/registry.ts:42 getNextStep — UNCHANGED for the linear default. With deps present, getNextStep stays array-order (deps constrain INVALIDATION, not execution order — execution remains the validated forward order). This keeps the change minimal: deps add a reverse-edge invalidation graph layered ON the existing linear forward walk, not a full scheduler rewrite.
- index.ts:31 barrel — rerunStep reachable via createWorkflow handle; export RerunSignal only if ctx.rerunSelf is built (mirror GotoSignal export index.ts:37).

Child-workflow-per-unit (recommendation b) needs NO source change — it already works: parent uses ctx.startChildWorkflow (context.ts:163), child-await/auto-resume at engine.ts:906-1005. Re-running one unit = re-invoke that one child engine.start with the same idempotency key; siblings untouched by construction.
design.mongokitUsage:
Moderate, all through the existing Repository extension (run.repository.ts:101 extends Repository<WorkflowRun>):

- The rerunStep state writes go through repository.updateOne (run.repository.ts:200) with the narrow steps.<i>.* $set map + CANCELLED_GUARD ({status:{$ne:'cancelled'}}) + {bypassTenant:true}, exactly like handleGoto (executor.ts:789). This inherits the schema-level {w:'majority',j:true} (run.model.ts:131) — durability preserved, no per-call write concern needed (mongokit has none; confirmed in the capability map).
- The run-level status flip done/failed→running uses assertAndClaim(RUN_MACHINE,...) / repo.claim() (the canonical CAS, run.repository.ts ~361) so two concurrent rerunStep calls on the same run race-resolve to one winner (null on loss = 'other caller won', not an error). RUN_MACHINE (status.ts:84) must permit done→running and failed→running transitions for rerun — add them or assertAndClaim throws before the CAS.
- No new collection, no change streams, no lease/lock needed for the single-node path (rerun is operator-triggered, not poll-driven). For the child-workflow-per-unit path, child re-invocation reuses the existing concurrency/idempotency machinery (partial-unique index run.model.ts:191 + create() E11000-catch run.repository.ts:155) so a double rerun of the same unit dedupes.
- mongokit's incrementIfBelow / leasePlugin / keyset pagination are NOT needed here — this is a graph/invalidation feature, not a capacity or scan feature.
design.durabilityModel:
Crash-durable + idempotent by reusing the engine's existing write-then-mirror discipline:

1) rerunStep persists the invalidation (target + dependents → pending, run → running) via a single narrow $set updateOne BEFORE re-arming the loop (DB-write-first, then in-memory mirror — invariant #1). If the process crashes after this write, stale-recovery (engine.ts:1264) or the next execute() simply finds a 'running' run with a 'pending' currentStepId and resumes exactly at the rerun target — identical to a fresh step claim. No special recovery path.
2) Re-execution of the rerun target is idempotent because the step goes through the normal 5-stage executeStep pipeline (executor.ts:213): conditional-skip → backoff → already-running → atomic-claim → handler. The atomic-claim CAS guarantees only one worker runs it; replay after crash re-claims cleanly.
3) Reusing-vs-recomputing dependents: cleared dependents have output=undefined, so any downstream step that reads ctx.getOutput(depId) recomputes from fresh upstream — correct dataflow. Non-dependents keep their persisted output (the checkpoint/memoization model) and are NEVER re-run.
4) The run-level done→running flip uses claim() CAS, so it is exactly-once at the transition level even under concurrent rerun calls (loser gets null).
5) Child-workflow-per-unit path: durability is the child run's own {w:'majority',j:true} doc; re-running a unit is starting a child with the same dedupe key — the partial-unique idempotency index makes a duplicate start return the race-winner instead of double-launching.
correctnessConcerns: checkNoProgress wedge (invariant #7): rerun target goes done→pending while currentStepId may equal a prior value; must register as progress or the run loop exits and the run hangs. Mirror goto's explicit escape hatch (engine.ts:857-860).

RUN_MACHINE / STEP_MACHINE transitions: done→running (run) and done→pending (step) must exist in status.ts:84-117 or assertAndClaim throws synchronously before the CAS. This is the #1 silent trap for new control-flow verbs.

Full-doc write hazard: rewindTo (engine.ts:1432) writes updateById(rewoundRun) — the whole hydrated doc. rerunStep MUST NOT copy that; mongoose-9 update-cast coerces embedded steps[] into Subdocuments in place and poisons the cache (engine.ts:1451-1463). Use narrow steps.<i>.* $set like handleGoto.

Concurrent rerun + in-flight step: if a dependent is currently 'running' on another worker when rerunStep resets it to 'pending', the heartbeat double-execution guard (executor.ts:440) and CANCELLED_GUARD must prevent a torn write. Safer: rerunStep should refuse (or wait) when any target-or-dependent step is 'running'; document that rerun targets a step whose downstream is quiescent.

Dependency-graph correctness: deps must be validated forward-only + acyclic at definition time (validation.ts). A forward ref or cycle makes getDependents loop or under-invalidate. Cheap topo check at createWorkflow.

Under-invalidation via hidden coupling: if a step reads shared context.X set by a sibling NOT in its declared deps, selective invalidation leaves it stale. This is a host responsibility — deps must declare TRUE dataflow. Document that ctx.set side-effects outside declared deps defeat selective rerun; recommend child-workflow-per-unit when units share no context.
Version pinning: rerun on a v1 run must resolve v1's engine/graph (registry.ts:80, engine.ts:607). Never let rerun rebind to the active definition or dependents are computed against the wrong graph.
Idempotency of the trigger itself: a double-clicked rerunStep must not double-invalidate mid-execution; the claim() done→running CAS makes the second call a null no-op.
design.philosophyNote: FITS — and the deps/child split is exactly what keeps it inside the primitives mandate. `deps: string[]` is pure STRUCTURE: it names which pre-registered code handler consumes which other handler's output. It ships NO executable logic as data, no prompt templates, no cost tables, no domain rules — it is the same category as the existing `condition`/`skipIf` knobs (a wiring declaration), just for invalidation edges instead of skip predicates. Selective re-run is a generic durable-execution primitive (DBOS/Restate-class), not a creative orchestrator. Cost accounting stays in the host (Prism's cost.ts): re-running a shot re-incurs cost in Prism's pipeline, the engine just re-arms the step. The recommendation is deliberately BOUNDED to fit: the engine gets the minimal DAG-invalidation layer (reverse-edge closure over declared deps), while the genuinely domain-shaped concern — 'a shot is an independently-rerunnable unit of work' — is pushed to the HOST via the existing child-workflow-per-unit pattern, which needs zero engine change. For Prism specifically (shots in a storyboard, order-lines in a saga), child-workflow-per-unit is the better primitive: each shot is already a natural sub-run, true isolation is free, and re-running one shot is just re-invoking one child with its idempotency key — no shared-context under-invalidation risk at all. So the verdict is: ship deps as the additive engine primitive for in-graph dataflow rerun, but recommend the host model child-fan-out units as children, and reach for engine-level deps only when units genuinely share one run's context. Reject any temptation to let the deps graph carry conditional/business expressions — that would cross into the orchestrator territory the mandate forbids.

verdict.area: execution graph + run lifecycle — rerunStep/rerunRun selective invalidation, deps DAG, executor forward-walk interaction, run.context dataflow channel, mongokit claim atomicity
correctnessHoles: P0 WEDGE — linear forward-walk over `done` non-dependents. The design keeps `getNextStep` as pure array `+1` (registry.ts:42) and leaves non-dependent intervening steps `done`. Trace A->B->C->D where D deps only on A: rerunStep('A') resets A,D to pending, leaves B,C done. Loop runs A, then moveToNextStep->getNextStep('A')=B. executeStep(B): not conditional, not retry, checkAlreadyRunning is null (B is `done`, not `running`), then claimStepExecution filter requires `steps.B.status $in ['pending','failed']` -> B is `done` -> modifiedCount===0 -> claim returns null -> getById returns run unchanged (currentStepId still B, status still running). checkNoProgress (engine.ts:846): currentStepId===prevStepId(B), status===prevStatus(done), prev not skipped, not retry-pending -> returns true -> shouldBreak. The run WEDGES at B in status=running with B done; D is NEVER reached. The design's own correctnessConcerns list does not contain this; its claim 'execution remains the validated forward order, deps add invalidation only' is simply false on this engine. There is no 'skip an already-done step and keep walking' path in the executor.

P0 DATAFLOW UNSOUNDNESS — `run.context` is the primary output channel, not StepState.output. Steps write results via `ctx.set(key,val)` -> persisted to `run.context.<key>` (context.ts:55-71). rerunStep invalidation only clears dependents' `StepState.output`; it does NOT revert any `run.context` keys the rerun target re-writes, and `deps:string[]` declares nothing about which context keys a step reads/writes. A non-dependent `done` step that previously derived a value from a context key the rerun target now overwrites is left stale and is 'NEVER re-run' by design. The whole dataflow-correctness argument rests on steps reading via ctx.getOutput(depId), but the idiomatic channel is ctx.set/run.context. The design files this under 'hidden coupling / host responsibility' as a footnote when it is the default failure mode for any workflow using ctx.set.

P0 CRASH WINDOW / LOST RERUN — split persistence between step-resets and run-flip. internalChanges writes the per-step resets via a narrow `$set` updateOne, and (separate bullet) flips run done->running via assertAndClaim/claim. If these are two writes: crash after step-resets land but before the run-flip leaves a TERMINAL run (status=done) with steps=pending. isTerminalState('done')===true (status.ts:156) so scheduler ignores it and execute() returns immediately — the rerun is SILENTLY LOST and the doc is structurally inconsistent. Reverse order leaves running+currentStepId=A while A is still done -> wedge. Recovery (engine.ts:1264) does not heal a run whose status is terminal.

Concurrent-rerun + in-flight dependent step. If a dependent is currently `running` on another worker when rerunStep flips it to pending, the heartbeat-abort guard + CANCELLED_GUARD do not prevent the still-running handler's success write (updateStepState done) from racing the reset. The design notes this but only as 'safer: refuse when running' — it must be a hard precondition (CAS-guarded), not advice.

Idempotency of the trigger relies on the run-flip CAS being a no-op on double-click, but because the step-resets are a separate non-CAS `$set`, a second rerunStep that loses the run-flip race can STILL have already executed its step-resets (or vice versa, ordering-dependent), double-clearing outputs mid-execution. Only a single atomic op closes this.

deps 'forward-only, earlier-in-array' validation conflicts with goto loop patterns. goto supports backward jumps (loops); in a loop a later step legitimately feeds an earlier one across iterations. A forward-only acyclic deps check at definition time cannot express the dataflow of any workflow that uses goto loops, so rerun-invalidation is undefined for the exact control-flow the engine already ships.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: Mostly FITS, with one caveat. `deps:string[]` is genuinely structure-as-data (names which pre-registered handler feeds which), same category as existing skipIf/condition wiring knobs — it ships no executable logic, no prompts, no cost tables. The child-workflow-per-unit recommendation correctly pushes the domain-shaped 'a shot is a rerunnable unit' concern to the host with zero engine change. Cost accounting stays in Prism's cost.ts. No orchestrator leak. CAVEAT: the design must hold the line that `deps` NEVER carries a conditional/business expression (it currently does not, good) — and given the run.context unsoundness, there is pressure to add 'declare which context keys this step reads' which would start to look like dataflow rules; that must be resisted or kept as pure key-name structure, not predicates.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: Correct primitives chosen but UNDER-USED for atomicity. claim() (Repository.ts:1477) accepts array `from` -> `$in` and operator patches ($set/$unset), so the ENTIRE invalidation (run done|failed->running PLUS all steps.i.status->pending, output/error/timestamps $unset, currentStepId set) can and MUST be expressed as ONE claim() CAS — that closes both the double-write crash window and the concurrent-rerun race in a single atomic op inheriting schema-level {w:majority,j:true}. The design instead describes a narrow `$set` updateOne for steps + a separate claim for the run flip, which is the non-idiomatic, race-prone shaping. No new collection/lease/incrementIfBelow needed — agreed. The claim from===to write-skip is irrelevant here. RUN_MACHINE already permits done|failed->running and STEP_MACHINE already permits done|failed->pending (status.ts:88-116) — so the design's claim that these transitions 'must be added or assertAndClaim throws' is WRONG; they exist. That reduces work but the design misstated the current state.
verdict.breakingVerdict: The 'minor-additive' claim is HONEST for the public surface as scoped: optional `deps?` on Step/StepConfig, optional `lastRerunAt?`/`rerunReason?` on StepState (Mixed/Date, no index), new engine.rerunStep verb, optional ctx.rerunSelf+RerunSignal export. Absent deps preserves the exact linear default. Two non-obvious risks: (1) the new forward-only acyclic deps validation in validateWorkflowDefinition would REJECT at createWorkflow time any existing v2.x workflow that (once it adopts deps) expresses a goto-loop dataflow — additive only if validation runs solely when deps is present (it does, acceptable). (2) If the wedge fix requires teaching getNextStep/the executor to skip `done` steps, THAT changes core forward-walk semantics and is potentially breaking for goto-replay and conditional-skip interactions — must be gated behind deps-present, or it is a silent behavior change. As written (getNextStep unchanged) it is non-breaking but also non-functional (wedges).
requiredFixes: Fix the forward-walk wedge: when cascade='dependents', the loop must SKIP intervening `done`/`skipped` non-dependent steps rather than try to claim them. Either (a) compute the invalidated set and have a deps-aware getNextStep advance to the next step whose status is pending/failed among target+dependents, or (b) reframe rerun to set currentStepId to the FIRST invalidated step and make moveToNextStep skip steps already `done`. Must be gated on deps-present so the linear default is untouched. Add a regression test for A->B->C->D, D deps A, rerun A reaches D without wedging at B.

Make the entire invalidation ONE atomic mongokit claim(): from:['done','failed'] -> to:'running', where:{no target-or-dependent step is `running`}, patch:{$set:{currentStepId:target, ['steps.i.status']:'pending' for each invalidated i, updatedAt}, $unset:{output/error/startedAt/endedAt/waitingFor/retryAfter for each invalidated i}}. Eliminates the two-write crash window, the lost-rerun-on-terminal-run hole, and the concurrent-rerun race in one shot. Do NOT use a separate narrow $set + separate run-flip.

Add a `where` precondition to that claim that fails (returns null) if ANY target-or-dependent step status === 'running', so rerun cannot reset an in-flight step. Document that rerun targets a quiescent subgraph. Loser/blocked => null, surfaced as a typed 'cannot rerun: step in flight' error, not a wedge.

Resolve the run.context dataflow unsoundness explicitly. Either (a) restrict rerun-with-deps to workflows that pass outputs ONLY via StepState.output (engine cannot enforce this — so document it as a hard precondition and prefer child-workflow-per-unit whenever ctx.set is used), or (b) drop the in-graph deps rerun entirely for context-using workflows and make child-workflow-per-unit the SOLE supported isolation primitive. Do not ship deps-rerun as 'generally correct' — it is only correct for the StepState.output-only subset.

Reconcile deps forward-only/acyclic validation with goto loops. Define behavior when a workflow uses both goto (backward jumps) and deps: either forbid deps on workflows containing backward gotos, or scope deps strictly to the acyclic forward segment. Specify it; do not leave getDependents to loop or under-invalidate.

Drop the false claim that RUN_MACHINE/STEP_MACHINE transitions must be added; they already permit done|failed->running and done|failed->pending. Update the design and instead add a test asserting they remain present.

checkNoProgress: verify (with a test) that the done->pending transition on the rerun target registers as progress. The existing escape hatch keys on the FROM-step being `skipped` (engine.ts:857-860); rerun does not mark anything skipped, so that hatch does NOT fire for rerun. Add an explicit rerun progress signal or the run breaks on the first iteration.

design.area: Per-step versioned output history (ring buffer) — engine state model + ctx primitive
design.summary: Add a bounded, append-only ring buffer of prior step outputs as a NEW field on StepState (never the single overwritten `output` slot), so a host can show and restore previous generations of a step (e.g. Prism regenerating a shot/voice clip). The success write at executor.ts:362 is the only place that produces a fresh output; that is where we $push the prior output into the history array with a $slice-bounded ring buffer in the SAME atomic update that sets the new output, so durability and idempotency are unchanged. A small additive ctx + repo surface (`ctx.outputHistory()`, `ctx.pinOutput(version)`) and a workflow/step-level `outputHistory` config knob expose read + restore. This makes streamline more capable as a durable substrate for the agentic/regeneration loops the checklist's Area 1 calls out, while staying a primitive: the engine stores and restores outputs but ships no domain/regeneration policy — the host decides when to keep history and which version to pin.
design.internalChanges:
1) Schema (storage/run.model.ts:10-30): add to StepStateSchema `outputHistory: { type: [Schema.Types.Mixed], default: undefined }` and `pinnedVersion: Number`. Mixed array mirrors the existing `output`/`stepLogs` pattern; `default: undefined` keeps the field absent on legacy/disabled runs (no migration, no index needed — it's read by _id).
2) Write path (execution/executor.ts:362-371, the success branch): when the step's resolved `outputHistory.keep > 0` AND a prior `output` exists, the SAME updateStepState call that sets the new `output:` must also push the prior output as a StepOutputVersion. Implement by extending buildStepUpdateOps (storage/update-builders.ts:116) to emit, alongside the `$set steps.<i>.output`, a `$push: { 'steps.<i>.outputHistory': { $each: [priorVersion], $slice: -keep } }`. $slice-negative is the ring-buffer bound — Mongo trims oldest in the same write. NOTE: this changes updateStepState/buildStepUpdateOps from pure-`$set/$unset` to also carry `$push`; normalizeUpdate already tolerates multi-operator docs, but applyStepUpdates (the in-memory mirror, update-builders.ts:140) must replicate the same push+slice so the mirror matches DB (cross-cutting invariant #1).
3) Restore path: WorkflowRunRepository.restoreStepOutput routes through the inherited mongokit `claim()`/`findOneAndUpdate` with CANCELLED_GUARD + bypassTenant, reading the chosen version and `$set`-ing it back into `steps.<i>.output` + `pinnedVersion`. ctx.pinOutput (context.ts, sibling to checkpoint at :245) calls it and updates the in-memory mirror, honoring `ctx.signal.aborted` before the write (invariant: every ctx I/O checks abort).
4) registry.rewindRun (workflow/registry.ts:89-117): leave as-is, but it currently drops the whole StepState incl. outputHistory on reset. Add an opt (`preserveHistory`) so a rewind-then-rerun can append the new generation rather than nuke prior ones — this is what makes "rerun produces a new history entry" work alongside the existing forward-cascade. Default false = current behavior.
5) No change to deriveRunStatus/STEP_MACHINE/checkNoProgress — history is metadata, not a status or control-flow signal, so none of the status-derivation or no-progress invariants are touched.
design.mongokitUsage: Leverages mongokit two ways, no new dependency. (a) The append is a `$push` with `$slice` issued through the existing repo.updateOne → inherited Repository.findOneAndUpdate (run.repository.ts:200), so audit/cache/tenant plugin hooks still fire and the schema-level {w:'majority',j:true} (run.model.ts:131) flows through unchanged — capability map §7/§1. (b) restoreStepOutput uses mongokit's `claim(id,{field:'status',from:['running','waiting','done'],where:{...}}, patch)` CAS so a concurrent cancel/recovery loses the race cleanly (null/modifiedCount:0 = another worker won, not an error) — capability map §7. We deliberately do NOT use mongokit pagination/lease/incrementIfBelow here: history is a small bounded inline array, not a queryable collection, so keyset pagination is irrelevant and there is no capacity gate. We also do NOT move write concern off the schema (capability map's load-bearing finding) — the $push inherits it automatically.
design.durabilityModel: Crash-durable because the prior output is folded into the SAME atomic findOneAndUpdate that records the new output (DB-write-first, then in-memory mirror — cross-cutting invariant #1). There is no separate write that could tear: either the step's new output AND its history entry both commit under {w:'majority',j:true}, or neither does. Idempotency: the history push happens only on the success transition (executor.ts:362), which runs once per successful attempt; on stale-recovery a step that already committed `done` is not re-run (executor's already-running/claim gates at executor.ts:213-251), so no duplicate entry. The ring buffer is self-bounding via `$slice:-keep`, so storage growth is hard-capped at `keep` versions per step regardless of crash/retry churn. Restore (ctx.pinOutput/restoreStepOutput) is a guarded CAS that is naturally idempotent — pinning the same version twice yields the same state and modifiedCount may be 0 the second time (treated as success, not error). Recovery path: outputHistory is plain run-doc data, reloaded with the run; no replay log to reconcile.
correctnessConcerns: Double-push on retry: the push must fire ONLY on the success-transition write (executor.ts:362), never on the running/heartbeat/checkpoint writes, or a flapping step inflates history. Gate strictly on the done-write and on the existence of a prior output.

In-memory mirror drift: applyStepUpdates (update-builders.ts:140) currently only handles $set/$unset on whole fields; it must replicate the $push+$slice ring semantics or the mirror diverges from DB and a later same-process read returns stale history (invariant #1).

Checkpoint/scatter collision: ctx.checkpoint writes steps.<i>.output = {__checkpoint} into the SAME slot (context.ts:255). The history push must never capture a __checkpoint sentinel as a 'prior output' — only the final committed output of a completed attempt is histor-worthy. Skip the push when the prior output carries __checkpoint.

Restore vs concurrent recovery: restoreStepOutput must be claim-guarded (status $ne cancelled + not mid-running on another worker) so a host-initiated pin can't clobber an output a stale-recovery worker is simultaneously rewriting.

goto/rewind interaction: rewindRun resets StepState and would discard outputHistory; the preserveHistory opt must be explicit so the host opts in, otherwise a rerun silently loses the very generations this feature exists to keep.

Unbounded value size: `keep` bounds COUNT, not byte-size. A step returning multi-MB outputs × keep can bloat the 16MB BSON doc. Document that history stores references/small payloads; large blobs belong in object storage with the run holding a handle (host responsibility, mirrors the broll/asset pattern).
design.philosophyNote: Fits — and was deliberately bounded to fit. It is a pure primitive: the engine stores the last N raw step outputs and can copy a chosen one back into the live slot. It ships ZERO domain logic — no notion of 'shot', 'voice clip', 'generation cost', or 'which version is better'. The host (Prism) decides when to enable history (`outputHistory.keep`), reads versions, and picks one to pin via the regenerate flow + cost.ts; the engine never auto-selects or scores. It is the storage/restore counterpart to the existing checkpoint/scatter primitives and respects every load-bearing invariant (single-doc atomic write, write-then-mirror, CANCELLED_GUARD, claim-CAS, write concern on schema). The one thing to actively reject: do NOT let this grow a 'regeneration policy', diff/merge of outputs, quality ranking, or auto-rollback-on-failure — those are host orchestration. Keep it to store-N + read + pin, or it crosses the primitives-not-orchestrator line.

verdict.area: Per-step versioned output history (ring buffer) — StepState field + ctx.outputHistory/pinOutput + restoreStepOutput
correctnessHoles: TRIGGER SEMANTICS ARE WRONG / NEARLY DEAD. The design says 'push the prior output when a fresh output is written (executor.ts:362) AND a prior output exists.' But on a normal forward run a step has NO prior committed output — `output` is unset until first success, and a `done` step is never re-run forward (getNextStep only advances). The ONLY paths that produce a SECOND success-write to the same slot are rewind/goto-back. So in practice history captures almost nothing unless the host reruns — yet the design pins capture to the success-write, not to the rerun. The capture point and the feature's purpose are misaligned; capture must be driven off the rerun/rewind transition, not 'prior output exists at success'.

PRIOR OUTPUT IS POLLUTED BY CHECKPOINT/SCATTER SENTINELS AND STALE RETRY STATE. `ctx.checkpoint`/`ctx.scatter` write `steps.<i>.output = { __checkpoint }` (context.ts:255) into the SAME slot during execution, and `claimStepExecution` (executor.ts:283-296) does NOT clear `output` on retry. So at the success write the 'prior output' is frequently a live `__checkpoint` sentinel or a stale value from a prior attempt of the SAME (not previous) generation. The design's 'skip if __checkpoint' patch is necessary but insufficient: a retried step whose previous attempt wrote a real (non-checkpoint) intermediate output would get that captured as a bogus 'version'. Capture must be gated on a genuine completed-generation boundary, not slot occupancy.

buildStepUpdateOps CANNOT SEE THE PRIOR OUTPUT. The design says 'extend buildStepUpdateOps to emit $push of priorVersion.' But buildStepUpdateOps(stepIndex, updates) only receives the NEW Partial<StepState>; the prior value lives on run.steps[i].output and is destroyed by applyStepUpdates (update-builders.ts:147) which runs in updateStepState BEFORE ops are built (executor.ts:650-652). The prior output must be snapshotted in updateStepState before applyStepUpdates, then threaded in — the 'just extend buildStepUpdateOps' framing is underspecified and would capture the already-overwritten value.

RESTORE ON TERMINAL (done) RUNS IS A NEW MUTABILITY HOLE. restoreStepOutput/pinOutput propose `from:['running','waiting','done']`. Mutating a `done` run's output post-completion (a) races the retention TTL sweeper (terminal+endedAt rows are TTL-purge candidates per retention.ts), (b) is not covered by the partial-unique idempotency index semantics which assume terminal = immutable, and (c) re-sets `updatedAt` but not `endedAt`, so a restored run looks freshly active to some queries but terminal to others. Either forbid restore on terminal runs or define explicit terminal-mutation semantics + TTL interaction.

preserveHistory RIDES THE ALREADY-BROKEN rewindTo WRITE PATH. rewindTo (engine.ts:1432) persists via `updateById(runId, rewoundRun)` — spreading the WHOLE run including the full steps[] array. That is exactly the mongoose-9 subdocument-coercion cache-poison hazard the cancel/pause paths explicitly avoid with narrow $set (invariant #8, engine.ts:1451-1463). Adding outputHistory to the field carried by this full-document overwrite means history preservation depends on a path that already violates the load-bearing narrow-$set invariant; under Mongoose 9 the spread can drop the new array field or poison the cache. preserveHistory must be implemented as a targeted $push/$set per step, not by widening the rewindRun full-doc spread.

IN-MEMORY MIRROR DRIFT (acknowledged but the fix is non-trivial). applyStepUpdates only does whole-field $set/$unset. It must replicate $push + $slice:-keep ring trimming in memory or a same-process getOutputHistory returns a mirror that diverges from DB (invariant #1). The negative-$slice trim-oldest must be reproduced exactly (drop from front when length>keep), and the mirror push of the PRIOR value must use the same snapshot, or a crash-recovery reload silently disagrees with what the host just read.

DOUBLE-PUSH UNDER STALE RECOVERY IS PLAUSIBLE AT THE COMMIT BOUNDARY. The design relies on 'done step is not re-run.' True for a committed `done`. But the crash window is: handler returns, updateStepState's atomic write commits the new output+push, THEN moveToNextStep runs as a SEPARATE write (executor.ts:374,716). If the process dies between those two writes, the step is `done` but currentStepId still points at it; stale-recovery reloads and the run-loop sees a done step — must confirm recovery advances rather than re-executing. If any recovery path can re-enter the handler for a step left mid-transition, the push fires twice. Needs an explicit idempotency guard on the push (e.g. only push when the new output differs from / supersedes the recorded attempt number), not just 'success runs once.'
verdict.philosophyVerdict: Mostly clean and the author was disciplined: the engine stores last-N raw outputs and copies one back — no 'shot', 'voice clip', cost, scoring, or regeneration policy leaks in. StepOutputVersion.reason carries 'success'|'rerun'|'restore' which is engine-intrinsic provenance, not domain logic — acceptable. The `pinnedVersion` field is the one smell: 'host-chosen index into history' is metadata the engine stores but never acts on, which is fine ONLY if the engine truly ignores it for control flow. Guard hard against the slippery slope the author themselves flagged: no diff/merge, no quality ranking, no auto-rollback-on-failure. As specified it respects primitives-not-orchestrator. The bounded-byte caveat (16MB BSON) is correctly pushed to the host. Verdict: philosophy-compliant if pinnedVersion stays inert and restore stays a dumb copy.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: Largely idiomatic. (a) The $push+$slice append correctly stays on schema-level writeConcern (run.model.ts:131) by routing through repo.updateOne→super.findOneAndUpdate — the capability map's load-bearing finding is respected; do NOT move WC per-op. (b) restoreStepOutput via mongokit claim() is correct: claim() supports array `from`→$in, AND-merged `where` predicates on dotted steps.<i>.* paths, and operator patches (Repository.ts:1499-1574), so the CANCELLED_GUARD + status CAS is expressible. One caveat: with array `from` the from===to no-op write-skip (Repository.ts:1531-1532) does NOT engage, so pinning the same version twice DOES issue a real write each time — idempotent in result but not write-free; fine, but don't claim it's a no-op. Mongokit is NOT forced here — the bounded inline array correctly avoids pagination/lease/incrementIfBelow, which would be wrong tools. No better primitive exists for an inline ring buffer; $push/$slice is the right Mongo idiom.
verdict.breakingVerdict: 'minor-additive' is ACCURATE for the additive surface: new optional StepState fields (default undefined → absent, no migration, read by _id), new opt-in config knob (keep:0/undefined = no behavior change), new ctx methods, new barrel type export, new repo verb. Type/runtime export split is honored. TWO honesty gaps: (1) buildStepUpdateOps / updateStepState change from pure-$set/$unset to also carrying $push — this is an INTERNAL contract change; any downstream that imported and relied on buildStepUpdateOps returning only {$set,$unset} (it IS exported per the audit's update-builders barrel mention) would see a shape change. That's at least a documented behavior note, arguably minor-breaking for that export. (2) restoreStepOutput mutating terminal `done` runs changes the de-facto 'terminal = immutable' contract other code/retention may assume — semantically breaking even if API-additive. Both must be called out in the migration note, not buried.
requiredFixes: Re-anchor the capture trigger: push a history version on the RERUN/REWIND transition (when a step that was previously `done` is reset and re-succeeds), NOT on 'a prior output happens to occupy the slot at success.' Capture the prior COMMITTED output snapshot in updateStepState before applyStepUpdates, and thread it into buildStepUpdateOps explicitly.

Gate the push to exclude __checkpoint sentinels AND stale intermediate outputs: only capture a value that represents a completed prior generation (e.g. prior StepState.status was 'done' with a real, non-__checkpoint output). Skip otherwise.

Add an explicit idempotency guard on the push keyed to the attempt/generation (e.g. record attempt number in StepOutputVersion and refuse to push if the top-of-buffer already has that attempt), to close the done-then-crash-before-moveToNextStep double-push window.

Fix applyStepUpdates to replicate $push + $slice:-keep ring semantics in memory (trim oldest from front when length>keep) using the same prior-output snapshot, so the mirror matches DB exactly (invariant #1).

Implement preserveHistory WITHOUT widening rewindRun's full-doc spread: persist the rewind via targeted per-step $set/$unset (or a dedicated repo verb) so it does not ride the updateById full-steps[] overwrite that violates the narrow-$set / mongoose-9 subdoc invariant (#8). This likely means fixing rewindTo's persistence regardless.

Decide and document restore-on-terminal policy: either forbid restoreStepOutput/pinOutput when run.status is terminal, OR define the TTL/endedAt/idempotency-index interaction explicitly (and likely re-open status to a non-terminal state on restore). Do not silently mutate a TTL-purge-eligible done run.

Document the internal-contract change to buildStepUpdateOps (now emits $push) and the terminal-mutability change in the migration note; add a test asserting the push fires exactly once per generation and never captures __checkpoint.

design.area: First-class declarative parallel + durable join (DAG fan-out/fan-in) for @classytic/streamline
design.summary: Add a declarative "branch group" primitive that fans out to N pre-registered branches and JOINs them durably with an all/any/race/allSettled completion policy that survives crashes. The honest design decision: do NOT build a new general DAG graph model — that would replace registry.getNextStep() (the linear-with-jumps core) and ripple through version-pinning, checkNoProgress, rewind, and migrateRun. Instead, implement the join as durable SUGAR over the two primitives that are already crash-durable: ctx.startChildWorkflow (each branch is a child run = a document, parked at zero compute) plus a new join-aware WaitSignal that parks the parent until the configured quorum of children reaches a terminal state. This keeps one fan-out concept in the engine (a run is a document), reuses the existing child-completion listener in handleWaitingState, and confines new code to additive seams. ctx.scatter stays the in-process, single-step, same-worker fan-out; the new ctx.joinBranches is the cross-step, cross-worker, crash-durable fan-out+join. This makes streamline cover agentic sub-agent fan-out/fan-in (Area 1) and parallel general workflows (Area 2) with real partial-failure semantics, not just in-memory Promise.all.
design.internalChanges:
Confined to additive branches in files the extension-point map already names; no rewrite of registry.getNextStep (the linear core is untouched).

1) context.ts (StepContextImpl): add joinBranches(), which — like startChildWorkflow at context.ts:163-170 and goto at :172-174 — does NOT write state; it throws a new WaitSignal('branchJoin', reason, {branches, policy, cancelLosers, parentRunId, parentStepId}). Add 'branchJoin' to the WaitSignal type union (context.ts:9). On resume, the executor re-invokes the handler with the join payload as input; joinBranches detects the resolved payload (input.__branchJoin) and returns it instead of re-throwing (same memoize-on-resume shape scatter uses with getCheckpoint).

2) executor.ts executeStepHandler catch (executor.ts:375-390): no change needed — WaitSignal already routes through handleWait, which persists waitingFor exactly-step. branchJoin rides the existing WaitSignal branch.

3) engine.ts handleWaitingState (engine.ts:871-1009): add a `stepState.waitingFor?.type === 'branchJoin'` branch modeled directly on the childWorkflow branch (:906-1005). On first entry: for each branch with no childRunId, look up childEngine via workflowRegistry.getEngine(workflowId), childEngine.start(input), and persist childRunId back via the narrow `$set: steps.<i>.waitingFor.data.branches.<j>.childRunId` (same write shape as :928-933). Then register ONE shared completion/failure listener set across both buses (reuse the exact dual-bus subscribe + `resumed` dedupe + cleanup pattern at :944-988), but instead of resuming on first child, it re-reads ALL child statuses and evaluates the quorum: all=every terminal & all done; any=≥1 done; race=≥1 terminal; allSettled=every terminal. Only when the quorum is met does it build JoinResult (reading each child's output/error via childEngine.get) and call this.resume(parentRunId, {__branchJoin: joinResult}). If cancelLosers and policy is race/any, call childEngine.cancel on still-running children after quorum. Returns false (async completion), same as childWorkflow.

4) checkNoProgress (engine.ts:846-865): NO change required — the parent transitions running→waiting on the WaitSignal (a real status change), so it is progress; and resume re-enters the same step which then moves forward via moveToNextStep. branchJoin never holds currentStepId constant without a status change, so it does not trip the no-progress trap (unlike goto).

5) cleanupEventListeners (engine.ts:194-220): the new listener set must register/teardown on all three prefixes like the childWorkflow path, or listeners leak across runs/tests.

6) define.ts / registry.ts / status.ts: UNCHANGED. No new step status, no new RUN_MACHINE/STEP_MACHINE transition (parent uses the existing running↔waiting↔running edges that childWorkflow already uses). getNextStep stays array-index +1. This is the load-bearing reason the change is non-breaking.
design.mongokitUsage: Leverages mongokit through the already-adopted Repository<WorkflowRun> seam — no new mongokit feature required, matching the "deepen adoption" map. (1) Persisting each child's childRunId and the parent park uses the existing repository.updateOne narrow `$set` path (run.repository.ts updateOne, routed so tenant/audit/cache plugins fire); these inherit the schema-level writeConcern {w:'majority',j:true} on run.model.ts:131 — durability is unchanged. (2) The parent run→waiting and resume→running transitions ride the existing assertAndClaim/claim() CAS the childWorkflow path already uses; no new state-machine edge. (3) Child runs are ordinary WorkflowRun docs created via childEngine.start → repo.create, so they get the partial-unique idempotency index + E11000 race-winner reuse for free (idempotent fan-out: re-entry after crash finds the already-started child by its idempotency key rather than double-spawning — REQUIRES callers pass a stable idempotency key per branch, see correctness). No change streams (mongokit has none); the join is event-bus + status re-read, not a tailing cursor. No incrementIfBelow/lease needed for the join itself.
design.durabilityModel: Checkpoint-resume, not replay — each branch IS a durable child run document; the parent is a durable WAITING document. Persistence points: (a) parent throws WaitSignal('branchJoin') → handleWait writes waitingFor.data (the full branch plan) exactly-step, write-first then mirror; (b) each child started → childRunId stamped back via narrow $set; (c) quorum met → this.resume(parent, {__branchJoin}) writes the resolved payload and advances. Recovery: on crash, the parent is found in WAITING with waitingFor.type='branchJoin'; the stale-recovery / handleWaitingState re-entry re-reads the branch plan, skips any branch that already has a childRunId (idempotent — no re-spawn), starts only missing children, and re-evaluates the quorum against current child statuses. Children recover independently via their own durable run loop (they are first-class runs). Idempotency: joinBranches memoizes on resume by detecting input.__branchJoin (mirror of scatter's getCheckpoint short-circuit) so the parent step's handler body never re-runs the post-join logic twice. Zero idle cost while parked — the parent holds no worker; it is just a document, satisfying the "wait indefinitely at zero compute" target.
correctnessConcerns: Double-spawn on crash between child.start() and the childRunId $set: must make child start idempotent. Require each branch to derive a deterministic idempotency key (e.g. parentRunId + ':' + stepId + ':' + key) so a re-entry after a mid-fan-out crash hits the partial-unique index (run.model.ts:191-200) and reuses the existing child instead of starting a duplicate. Without this, recovery re-runs uncommitted branch starts.

Lost wake / listener-only race: handleWaitingState's child-completion is an in-process bus listener. A child that completes on another worker (or completes before the parent subscribes after a crash) won't fire the parent's local listener. Mitigation: on every parent re-entry, re-read all child statuses synchronously and evaluate the quorum BEFORE relying on the listener (the listener is an optimization, the status re-read is the correctness path) — and let the scheduler's stale/waiting sweep periodically re-enter parked branchJoin parents so a missed event is eventually reconciled.

Resume idempotency / double-resume: the dual-bus listener must keep the existing `resumed` dedupe flag, AND this.resume must be CAS-guarded (waiting→running) so two near-simultaneous quorum evaluations (e.g. last two children finishing together) cannot both advance the parent. claim() returning null on the loser is the correct, non-error outcome.

Partial quorum + cancelLosers ordering: for race/any, cancel still-running losers only AFTER the parent has durably resumed, never before — cancelling first then crashing would drop the winner's result. Cancellation of losers must tolerate already-terminal children (no-op).

allSettled vs all failure semantics: 'all' with one failed child must surface as a failed join (parent step fails → saga compensation can fire), while 'allSettled' must resolve with per-branch status and NEVER fail the parent. Map these explicitly in JoinResult.satisfied; do not let a child failure event implicitly fail the parent except under 'all'/'any'(zero-success)/'race'(failed-first).

Cancellation propagation: cancelling the parent run must walk waitingFor.data.branches and cancel children (explicit tree walk — the map notes this is [M~], not automatic). Add this to the parent's cancel path or document that orphaned children TTL-out.

Listener leak: register+teardown on all three event prefixes (container bus / globalEventBus / signalStore) via cleanupEventListeners or children parked cross-container leak handlers across runs/tests.
design.philosophyNote: Fits the primitives-not-orchestrator rule, but ONLY because it was deliberately bounded to sugar over existing durable primitives rather than a new graph engine. The branch plan is pure wiring data: it SELECTS pre-registered child workflows by workflowId and maps their inputs — it never ships executable logic, prompts, cost tables, or domain rules as data (the named-handler-registry constraint). All creative/business logic stays in the child workflows the host registers via createWorkflow, and cost accounting stays in Prism's pipelines/cost.ts. Two guardrails kept it in bounds: (1) I rejected the tempting full declarative DAG — replacing registry.getNextStep would pull serialized graph topology + condition strings into the engine (exactly the dead workflowDefinition.model.ts condition:String trap) and risk it becoming a data-driven orchestrator; the linear-with-jumps core is left untouched. (2) The join is expressed as one new WaitSignal kind + one handleWaitingState branch that reuses the child-workflow machinery, so the engine gains a control-flow PRIMITIVE (durable fan-out+join with a completion policy), not an opinion about WHAT runs in the branches. If a reviewer wanted the richer 'declared graph with serialized branch conditions' version, that should be REJECTED and kept in the host/agent layer — only the policy-based join over named child workflows belongs in the engine.

verdict.area: First-class declarative parallel + durable join (DAG fan-out/fan-in) via ctx.joinBranches over child workflows + a 'branchJoin' WaitSignal
correctnessHoles: DEAD-WAIT ON CRASH (the killer). A 'branchJoin' parent parks in status:'waiting' with waitingFor.type:'branchJoin' and NO resumeAt/timer. I verified the only scheduler recovery sweeps are staleRunning (status:'running' + stale heartbeat), readyToResume (waiting + waitingFor.type:'timer' + resumeAt<=now), and readyForRetry (waiting + retry-ready) in query-builder.ts:98-178. A branchJoin parent matches NONE of them. recoverStale (engine.ts:1275) only claims from:'running'. So after a process restart the in-process bus listener that is the SOLE wake path is gone and nothing re-enters handleWaitingState for that parent. The run waits forever. The design's mitigation (concern #2: 'let the scheduler stale/waiting sweep periodically re-enter parked branchJoin parents') describes a sweep that does NOT exist, and the internalChanges change-list never adds a query or smart-scheduler poll branch to create it. The existing childWorkflow primitive has this same latent bug; the proposal inherits AND amplifies it (N children = N missable wakes).

NO DURABLE PARENT BACK-POINTER. The wake is entirely event-bus 'workflow:completed' emit heard by a live listener (engine.ts:983-988). A child that finishes (a) on another worker/container, or (b) during the parent-crash window before the parent re-subscribes, fires no parent listener. The child stores parentRunId/parentStepId in the PARENT's waitingFor.data, but the child run itself has no stored pointer and its completion path performs no durable parent-notify (no $set on the parent, no signalStore publish keyed to parent). Reconciliation therefore cannot be event-driven across processes; it MUST be a polling re-read, which is the missing sweep above.

DOUBLE-SPAWN WINDOW IS REAL AND THE FIX IS NOT WIRED. Crash between childEngine.start() (mirrors engine.ts:923) and the childRunId $set (engine.ts:928-932) leaves a branch with no childRunId. IF re-entry ever happens, that branch re-spawns. The design says require a deterministic idempotency key per branch, but the proposed handleWaitingState wiring calls childEngine.start(input) with NO idempotencyKey (exactly like the existing branch). start() DOES accept idempotencyKey (engine.ts:448), but the engine, not the caller, issues start() here — so the key must be synthesized inside handleWaitingState (e.g. parentRunId:stepId:branchKey). That synthesis is absent from the change list; it is only stated as a caller contract it cannot enforce.

PARENT-CANCEL ORPHANS CHILDREN. Verified cancel() (engine.ts:1445-1474) sends a narrow $set and does NOT walk waitingFor.data.branches. Cancelling a branchJoin parent leaves every child run executing (burning LLM compute) until TTL. cancelLosers for race/any has the same gap. The design flags this [M~] but leaves it unresolved ('add to cancel path or document orphaned children TTL-out').

RESUME-PAYLOAD MEMOIZATION COLLISION. joinBranches detects input.__branchJoin to short-circuit on resume, mirroring scatter's getCheckpoint. But scatter stores under steps[i].output.__checkpoint, while the join resolves via resume(parent,{__branchJoin}) which flows as the step's resume PAYLOAD, not output. If a branch child is itself a workflow that can resume the parent with a user payload, or if a step both scatters and joins, the __branchJoin / __childFailed sentinels share the untyped payload channel with no namespacing guard. Needs an explicit, reserved, collision-proof sentinel and a check that the handler body cannot observe it.

QUORUM RACE ON SIMULTANEOUS COMPLETION. Two last children completing near-simultaneously each trigger a quorum re-eval; the design relies on the 'resumed' flag + CAS-guarded resume. The 'resumed' flag is per-listener-closure in-memory and does NOT survive across workers — only the waiting->running claim() CAS is the real guard. That is correct as a guard, but the JoinResult is BUILT (reading each child via childEngine.get) BEFORE the claim, so both evaluators do the full read; only fine if the loser discards on null-claim. Must assert the loser path is a no-op, not an error/double-advance.

allSettled / all FAILURE MAPPING leaks into engine semantics. 'all with one failed child => fail parent' vs 'allSettled never fails parent' is a completion-policy decision the engine must encode in deriveRunStatus-adjacent logic. This is borderline domain policy living in the engine; acceptable as a fixed enum of 4 policies but must be implemented as pure status math, not configurable predicates.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: Mostly compliant; the 4-policy completion enum is the only domain-adjacent surface and is acceptable only if frozen as pure status math, never a serialized predicate.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: Idiomatic and minimal — correctly a 'deepen adoption' change, not a new mongokit feature. Reuses repository.updateOne narrow $set (childRunId stamping, schema-level {w:'majority',j:true} on run.model.ts:131 preserved) and the existing claim()/assertAndClaim CAS for waiting<->running. No change streams (mongokit has none) — correct. HOWEVER the design under-uses the one mongokit primitive that would close the biggest hole: the missing reconciliation sweep should be a new CommonQueries entry (waiting + waitingFor.type:'branchJoin' + a due_at-style nextReconcileAt) fed through repo.queryLean/cursor exactly like staleRunning, and the per-branch idempotent child start must pass options.idempotencyKey into start() so the partial-unique index + E11000 race-winner reuse (engine.ts:486-489) does the dedupe for free. Both are existing mongokit-backed seams the proposal names but does not actually wire.
verdict.breakingVerdict: The 'minor-additive' claim is HONEST at the type/API level: new optional ctx method, new WaitSignal literal, new JoinResult type, no STEP_MACHINE/RUN_MACHINE edges, getNextStep untouched, no removed exports. isolatedModules type/value export split is respected. BUT it ships a NEW durable on-disk state shape (waitingFor.type:'branchJoin' with a branch-plan payload) that older deployed engine versions in a mixed-version fleet cannot interpret — an in-flight run parked as branchJoin will be unrecognized by a v2.3.x worker and fall through handleWaitingState to return false (silent dead-wait), bypassing migrateRun. That is a forward-compat data hazard, not a code breaking change, and must be called out in the migration note: do not roll out branchJoin-producing code until all workers understand the new wait type.
requiredFixes: Add the durable reconciliation path BEFORE shipping: a new CommonQueries query (status:'waiting' + waitingFor.type:'branchJoin', notPaused, with a nextReconcileAt<=now field) + a getReadyBranchJoins repo method + a new poll branch in smart-scheduler.poll(). On re-entry it must re-read ALL child statuses synchronously and evaluate quorum (status re-read is the correctness path; the bus listener is only an optimization). Without this the feature dead-waits on any crash. This also fixes the pre-existing childWorkflow latent bug.

Make child start idempotent inside handleWaitingState: synthesize a deterministic idempotencyKey = `${parentRunId}:${parentStepId}:${branchKey}` and pass it to childEngine.start({...}, { idempotencyKey }) so the start()/childRunId-$set crash window cannot double-spawn (E11000 returns the race-winner). Do not leave this as a caller contract — the engine issues the start.

Stamp a durable parent back-pointer on each child at start (e.g. child.meta.parentRunId/parentStepId) AND have the child completion path publish to signalStore keyed to the parent (cross-process), so reconciliation is not solely dependent on the in-process bus.

Implement parent-cancel child-tree-walk: cancel() (and the cancelLosers branch) must read waitingFor.data.branches and call childEngine.cancel on each non-terminal child, tolerant of already-terminal (no-op). Otherwise cancel orphans N running child workflows.

Reserve a collision-proof, namespaced resume sentinel (not bare __branchJoin / __childFailed on the untyped payload) and guard that the handler body can never observe it; ensure scatter's __checkpoint slot and the join payload channel cannot interfere when a step uses both.

Confirm the quorum-eval loser path: JoinResult build happens after the waiting->running claim() wins, or the loser strictly discards on null-claim — assert no double-advance and no error on the loser.

Implement the 4 completion policies as pure status math (closed enum), never as serialized/configurable predicates; map all->fail-parent-on-any-failure, allSettled->never-fail, any/race per spec, explicitly in JoinResult.satisfied.

Add a forward-compat migration note: in a mixed-version fleet, a v2.3.x worker hitting waitingFor.type:'branchJoin' falls through to return false (silent dead-wait). Gate rollout so all workers understand the new wait type before any branchJoin run is created.

Register/teardown the join listener set on all three event prefixes (container bus / globalEventBus / signalStore) via cleanupEventListeners, matching the childWorkflow path, or listeners leak across runs/tests.

design.summary: Add a single bounded primitive — `createWorkflowFromDefinition(definitionDoc, handlerRegistry)` — that hydrates an existing `WorkflowDefinitionDoc` (already in `definition.model.ts`, already exported, currently dead storage) into a runnable `Workflow` by SELECTING pre-registered in-code named handlers and wiring their I/O declaratively. The JSON definition may only reference handler names, declare per-step retries/timeout, name a registered predicate for `condition`/`skipIf`/`runIf`, and declare static input wiring (which prior step outputs feed a step) — it ships NO executable code. This makes streamline able to publish/version workflow TEMPLATES and let a host assemble pipelines from data while keeping every line of logic in compiled code the host registered. It deliberately stops short of an orchestrator: no expression language, no serialized functions, no embedded branching DSL. The honest recommendation is to ship the named-handler/predicate-selection slice and HARD-REJECT the serialized-`condition`-string field that the model ships today (deprecate it, never deserialize it).
design.internalChanges:
Hydration is a thin adapter in a NEW file `workflow/from-definition.ts` that produces the exact same inputs `createWorkflow` already consumes — it does NOT touch the run loop, executor, or storage CAS. Concretely:

- `from-definition.ts`: maps `def.steps[]` (DefinitionStepRef) into the in-memory `WorkflowConfig.steps` record by LOOKING UP each `handler`/`conditionRef`/`skipIfRef`/`runIfRef`/`compensateRef` in the supplied `HandlerRegistry`. Missing key -> throw `UnknownHandlerError` immediately. It then calls the existing `createWorkflow(def.workflowId, normalizedConfig)` so all downstream wiring (engine, registry, container, version pin) is unchanged.
- `define.ts:138 toStepDef`: already threads condition/skipIf/runIf onto `Step` — reused verbatim; hydration just supplies real fn references resolved from names. No change to `Step` interface (`core/types.ts:4-93`) needed for the bounded slice.
- `workflow/registry.ts`: UNCHANGED. `getNextStep` (array index +1), `createRun` (pins `definitionVersion` from `def.version`), and `rewindRun` all operate on the hydrated definition identically to a code-defined one. This is why durability is free — hydration produces an ordinary `WorkflowDefinition` + `WorkflowHandlers`.
- `definition.model.ts`: ADD `handler: String` (required-at-app-level) and the optional `*Ref` string fields to the `steps[]` subschema; DEPRECATE the existing `condition: String` field (keep for back-compat read, never deserialize). Add a JSDoc banner: "condition is dead/legacy — use conditionRef + a registered predicate."
- `index.ts:58-61`: the `workflowDefinitionRepository` is already exported; add exports for `createWorkflowFromDefinition`, `loadWorkflowFromStore`, `HandlerRegistry` (type), `DefinitionStepRef` (type), and the two error classes. Keep `export type` vs `export` split for isolatedModules.

No changes to engine.ts, executor.ts, status.ts, run.repository.ts, or retention.ts.
design.mongokitUsage: Light, read-only. The definition store (`workflowDefinitionRepository` in `definition.model.ts`) is currently a hand-rolled object NOT extending mongokit's `Repository`. Recommended bounded improvement: leave the run/CAS path entirely alone (it already extends `Repository<WorkflowRun>`), but optionally migrate the DEFINITION repo to `extends Repository<WorkflowDefinitionDoc>` so reads route through mongokit plugin hooks and `getActiveDefinitions`/`getVersionHistory` can use mongokit keyset pagination (`PaginationEngine.stream`, repo-core-owned) for large template catalogs. The version-resolution read (`getByVersion`/`getLatestVersion`) is a plain indexed `findOne` on the existing `{ workflowId, version }` unique index (`definition.model.ts:100`) — no claim/CAS needed because definitions are immutable-per-version templates, not runs. mongokit's `claim`/`incrementIfBelow`/`leasePlugin` are NOT used here — hydration creates no durable state of its own. Write concern is irrelevant for definition reads; the run schema's `{w:'majority',j:true}` (`run.model.ts:131`) continues to govern all run writes unchanged.
design.durabilityModel: Hydration adds ZERO new durable state and ZERO new crash-recovery path — that is the whole point and the safety argument. A workflow built via `createWorkflowFromDefinition` is byte-for-byte equivalent at runtime to one built via `createWorkflow`: same `WorkflowRegistry`, same `createRun` stamping `definitionVersion = def.version` (`registry.ts:80`), same DB-write-first executor, same `assertAndClaim` CAS, same heartbeat/stale-recovery. Idempotency is inherited unchanged: the run's partial-unique index + E11000-catch in `create()` (`run.model.ts:191-200`) still governs start dedupe. Crash mid-run resolves via `versionRegistry.lookupVersion(workflowId, definitionVersion)` exactly as today — PROVIDED the host re-hydrates and re-registers the same definition version on worker restart. The only new operational requirement: handler registries must be deterministic and rebuilt identically on every worker (they are code, loaded at boot), and the same `(workflowId, version)` JSON must resolve to the same handler names. Recovery path is the existing one; no new sweeper, no new state field, no new `recoveryAttempts` interaction.
correctnessConcerns: Registry/definition drift across workers: if worker A hydrates v1.2.0 mapping step `charge`->handlerX and worker B's registry maps `charge`->handlerY, a resumed run silently executes different code. Mitigation: hydration must validate that every step's `handler` name resolves, and the host SHOULD pin a content hash of (handler-name set + version) — recommend an optional `def.handlerSignature` checked at hydrate so a drifted registry throws instead of mis-executing. This mirrors the existing version-pinning contract (registry.ts:80, engine.ts:607-611).

Unknown-handler must fail CLOSED at hydration time, never at step-execution time. Resolving a missing handler lazily inside the executor would make a run fail mid-flight after side effects — throw `UnknownHandlerError` synchronously in `createWorkflowFromDefinition` before any run can start.

Version-graph stability: a published template v2 that reorders/renames steps must keep step IDs stable or rely on the existing `migrateRun` hook (engine.ts:268-315). `getNextStep` is pure array order (registry.ts:42), so step ORDER in the JSON is load-bearing for in-flight runs — document that reordering steps is a breaking template change requiring a new version + migrateRun, not an edit-in-place.

No-progress invariant is untouched because hydration adds no new control-flow verb — `checkNoProgress` (engine.ts:846-865) sees ordinary sequential steps. Do NOT extend this primitive into branching/goto-from-data; that would require teaching checkNoProgress a new signal and crosses into orchestrator territory.

Predicate purity: named predicates (conditionRef/skipIf/runIf) run on the hot path; a host-registered predicate that does I/O or is non-deterministic re-evaluates on resume and can flip a skip decision. Document predicates must be pure sync/over-context functions, same contract as code-defined StepConfig.condition.

Mutable definition docs: `workflowDefinitionRepository.update` (definition.model.ts:241) can mutate a published version in place — for templates this is unsafe for in-flight runs. Recommend treating (workflowId, version) as immutable post-publish and only ever creating new versions.
design.philosophyNote: FITS — but only because it is bounded to selection + wiring, with logic kept exclusively in host-compiled code. The HARD RULE is honored: JSON SELECTS which pre-registered handlers run and declares their retry/timeout/order/predicate-by-name; it ships no executable logic. The existing `condition: String` field in `definition.model.ts:60` ("serialized condition function") is the part that VIOLATES the rule — it is an invitation to `eval`/`new Function` a stored string, which would turn the engine into a data-driven orchestrator hosting business logic. I recommend REJECTING that field outright: deprecate it, never write a deserializer for it, and replace it with `conditionRef` (a name into a host predicate map). Cost accounting, prompt templates, and domain branching stay in the host (Prism's pipelines/cost.ts and its handler implementations) — the registry only ever holds references to them. The feature is also additive and risk-free to durability because it produces an ordinary in-memory `WorkflowDefinition`+`WorkflowHandlers` and reuses the entire existing run loop unchanged. If a reviewer wanted to be conservative, shipping ONLY `createWorkflowFromDefinition` (handler+predicate name selection, no I/O-wiring metadata) is the minimal defensible slice; anything richer (expression DSL, data-driven branching, serialized conditions) should be rejected as orchestrator creep that belongs in the agent layer, per the project's 'Prism = primitives, agent = workflow' rule.

correctnessHoles: REGISTRY/DEFINITION DRIFT IS WORSE THAN THE DESIGN ADMITS, because of WeakRef last-write-wins on (workflowId, version). engine constructor calls workflowRegistry.register (engine.ts:375) which does versionedEngines.set(workflowId@version, WeakRef(engine)) with NO conflict check. If worker process hydrates the SAME (workflowId, v1.2.0) JSON twice against DIFFERENT registries (e.g. a buggy deploy where handler map for 'charge' changed but the host forgot to bump the version), the second createWorkflowFromDefinition silently OVERWRITES the first engine in the global map. A crash-resumed v1.2.0 run then routes via lookupVersion (engine.ts:608) to whichever engine won the last register() race — executing different code than it started under, with ZERO error. The design's proposed `handlerSignature` mitigation is OPTIONAL ('SHOULD pin a content hash') — it must be MANDATORY and the engine must REJECT a second register() for the same (workflowId, version) whose signature differs, or this is a silent-wrong-execution hole that the existing code-defined path doesn't have (code is identical across workers by construction; hydrated-from-data is not).

CRASH WINDOW: createWorkflowFromDefinition produces NO durable record of WHICH definition doc + handler-name set a run was started against. createRun (registry.ts:80) pins only definitionVersion (a string like '1.2.0'), not the definition content nor the handler-name->identity binding. On worker restart the host MUST re-hydrate the exact same JSON AND re-register the exact same handler names; if the host loads definition v1.2.0 from the store but the stored doc was mutated in place (workflowDefinitionRepository.update at definition.model.ts:241 allows this — there is no immutability guard), the resumed run walks a DIFFERENT step graph. getNextStep (registry.ts:42) is pure array-index, so a single reordered/inserted step in the mutated doc silently mis-sequences an in-flight run with no StepNotFoundError if IDs still resolve. The design names this ('Mutable definition docs') but files it as a soft 'Recommend' rather than a required guard.

CONTEXTFACTORY IS A RED HERRING THAT INTRODUCES A FALSE DURABILITY ASSUMPTION. Verified: createContext is invoked ONLY at start (registry.ts:69) and at scheduled-draft creation (scheduling.service.ts:328) — NEVER on resume/recovery; resume reuses the persisted run.context. So a named contextFactory in the registry only matters on the starting worker. The design lists contextFactories in HandlerRegistry and a contextFactory opt as if it participates in recovery determinism — it does not. Worse, if the host believes the named contextFactory must match across workers for recovery (per durabilityModel's 'rebuilt identically on every worker' claim), they will over-constrain; the real determinism requirement is ONLY handlers + predicates, not context factories. Misstating which pieces are load-bearing for recovery is itself a correctness risk.

PREDICATE NON-DETERMINISM ON RESUME is real and under-weighted. checkConditionalSkip runs step.condition/skipIf/runIf on EVERY executeStep pass (executor.ts:228, conditional.ts:19-21), including after stale-recovery re-claim. A named predicate resolved from data is no more pure than a code one, but the data path makes it trivially easy for a host to wire a predicate that reads run.context fields that a PRIOR step mutated — flipping a skip decision between the original run and the recovery re-run, causing a step to execute once on attempt-1 and skip on recovery (or vice versa). This is the same hazard as code-defined, but the selection-by-name ergonomics invite hosts to treat predicates as cheap config. Hydration MUST validate predicate names resolve, but cannot validate purity — needs an explicit doc-contract + ideally a dev-mode double-eval check.

NO ATOMICITY BETWEEN 'definition fetched' AND 'run started'. loadWorkflowFromStore does getByVersion (a plain findOne, definition.model.ts:201) then hydrates then start(). Between the read and the run's create(), nothing pins the definition; if getLatestVersion is used (the design's convenience path) and a new version is published in that window, two near-simultaneous starts can bind to different 'latest' versions — acceptable for templates, but the design should force version-explicit loads for any run that must be reproducible, not default to 'latest'.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: FITS the hard rule, with one mandatory carve-out the design already gets right. The named-handler/predicate-by-name registry is pure SELECTION + wiring: JSON references compiled handler names, declares retries/timeout/order, names predicates — it ships no executable logic. Verified against source that step.condition is invoked as a real function (conditional.ts:21) and the DB `condition: String` field (definition.model.ts:60) is genuine DEAD STORAGE — never deserialized, no eval/new Function anywhere in src. The design's HARD-REJECT of that serialized-condition string (deprecate, never write a deserializer, replace with conditionRef) is exactly correct and is the single most important philosophy call here: hydrating that string would turn the engine into a data-driven business-logic orchestrator. Watch items that must stay OUT: (1) no `inputs` I/O-wiring metadata beyond opaque pass-through — the design says handlers still read via ctx.getOutput, which is fine, but any future 'map output.foo -> input.bar' expression is orchestrator creep; the minimal defensible slice (handler+predicate name selection only, drop the inputs metadata) is the safer ship. (2) No expression language, no data-driven branching — correctly excluded. Cost/prompt/domain rules stay in the Prism host's handler implementations and cost.ts; the registry holds only references. Verdict: bounded primitive, philosophy-clean IF shipped as the minimal slice and IF the condition-string rejection is enforced.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: Mostly correct and idiomatic, but built on ONE FALSE PREMISE that must be flagged. The mongokit capability map asserts streamline 'already depends on mongokit ^3.14.0 + repo-core ^0.5.0'. FALSE in this tree: streamline/package.json dependencies are ONLY {luxon, semver} — @classytic/mongokit and @classytic/repo-core are NOT declared, yet run.repository.ts:22-32 imports them and extends Repository<WorkflowRun>. That is a pre-existing undeclared-dependency packaging bug (works only because the monorepo hoists them); it is orthogonal to this feature but any PR touching storage should add the explicit deps. On the feature itself the mongokit usage is correct and appropriately LIGHT: hydration creates zero durable state, touches no CAS/claim path, leaves run.repository.ts and the schema-level {w:'majority',j:true} (run.model.ts:131) untouched — all correct. The optional suggestion to migrate workflowDefinitionRepository (currently a hand-rolled plain object, definition.model.ts:167, NOT extending Repository) to `extends Repository<WorkflowDefinitionDoc>` is idiomatic and would gain plugin hooks + keyset PaginationEngine.stream for large template catalogs (repo-core-owned types, already the pattern in run.repository.ts). getByVersion is a plain indexed findOne on the existing {workflowId, version} unique index (definition.model.ts:100) — no claim/CAS needed since definitions are immutable-per-version; correct call. Do NOT reach for claim/incrementIfBelow/leasePlugin here — design correctly avoids them.
verdict.breakingVerdict: Claim of 'minor-additive' is HONEST for the runtime API surface but INCOMPLETE on the schema. New exports (createWorkflowFromDefinition, loadWorkflowFromStore, types, two error classes) are purely additive — safe minor. Reusing createWorkflow/toStepDef unchanged means zero engine/executor/storage diff — confirmed feasible from source. BUT: (1) Adding `handler: String` to the WorkflowDefinitionSchema.steps subschema (definition.model.ts:54) as 'required-at-app-level' is fine ONLY if it stays schema-optional; if it is ever made Mongoose-required it breaks reads of every EXISTING stored definition doc (which have no handler field) — a silent breaking change for anyone using the doc store today. The design says 'required-at-app-level' which is correct, but the migration note must explicitly state the schema field stays optional and validation happens at hydrate time. (2) Deprecating `condition: String` is non-breaking for reads (kept for back-compat) — honest. (3) The undeclared mongokit/repo-core deps mean this 'published npm package' may already be broken for external consumers who don't hoist; not caused by this PR but must not be made worse. Net: minor-additive is defensible with an explicit 'schema field stays optional, no Mongoose-required, no deserializer for condition' migration note.
requiredFixes: Make handlerSignature MANDATORY, not optional. createWorkflowFromDefinition must compute a deterministic signature over (sorted step IDs + their resolved handler/predicate/compensation NAMES + version) and stamp it. The global registry register() (engine.ts:114) must REJECT (throw) a second registration for the same (workflowId, version) whose signature differs — closing the WeakRef last-write-wins silent-mis-execution hole. Hydration must also store this signature on the run at createRun so a resumed run can assert the re-hydrated definition matches what it started under, failing closed (VERSION_MISMATCH-style) on mismatch.

Enforce definition immutability per (workflowId, version). Either block workflowDefinitionRepository.update (definition.model.ts:241) for the steps[] array post-publish, or have createWorkflowFromDefinition refuse to hydrate a doc whose content hash doesn't match a stored published hash. getNextStep is pure array order (registry.ts:42), so an in-place step reorder silently mis-sequences in-flight runs with no error when IDs still resolve.

Throw UnknownHandlerError SYNCHRONOUSLY at hydrate for every unresolved handler/conditionRef/skipIfRef/runIfRef/compensateRef name — before any run can start.
