Verified the executor resolves handlers by ID at runtime (registry.ts:35, getHandler), so a lazily-missing handler would fail mid-run after side effects. Hydration must walk all refs eagerly.

Remove contextFactories/contextFactory from the durability-determinism story (or document precisely that they matter ONLY at start, never on resume — verified createContext is start-only, registry.ts:69 + scheduling.service.ts:328). Correct durabilityModel to state that ONLY handler + predicate name->identity bindings must be stable across workers; context is persisted and reused on resume.

Default loadWorkflowFromStore to version-EXPLICIT loads; do not silently resolve 'latest' for runs that must be reproducible. If a latest-resolving convenience is offered, it must resolve the concrete version once and pin it before start.

Add explicit @classytic/mongokit ^3.14.0 and @classytic/repo-core dependencies to streamline/package.json (pre-existing undeclared-dependency bug surfaced by this review; the design's mongokit suggestions assume they're declared). Add the recommended test asserting Model.schema.options.writeConcern stays {w:'majority',j:true}.

Ship the MINIMAL slice only: handler + predicate + compensation name selection, plus retries/timeout/order. Drop the `inputs` I/O-wiring metadata field for v1 — it is the nearest edge to orchestrator creep and the design itself flags it as the conservative cut. Add it later only if a concrete need appears.

Document predicate purity as a hard contract (pure, sync-or-over-context, no I/O, no reads of fields a prior step mutated within the same run) and add a dev-mode double-evaluation check to catch flip-on-resume. Same contract as code-defined StepConfig.condition, but the data ergonomics make violations likelier.

design.area: Agentic-workflow primitives (dynamic step injection, durable tool-call loop, sub-agent fan-out/join, agent-managed human gates, streaming progress)
design.summary: Five additive, neutral primitives that make streamline "smart for agenting" without putting any LLM client, prompt, or domain logic in the engine. (1) ctx.injectSteps() lets a handler grow the run's graph at runtime by appending named, pre-registered handlers into a PER-RUN step overlay — never mutating the shared version-pinned definition. (2) ctx.loop() is a durable call->observe->decide iterator: each iteration's agent-supplied decision is checkpointed so a crash resumes mid-loop without re-calling the tool/LLM. (3) ctx.spawnAgents()/join builds durable sub-agent fan-out on top of the existing childWorkflow wait + scatter checkpoint machinery, with explicit cancel-propagation. (4) ctx.gate() is a human-approval waitpoint an agent can open and that engine.resolveGate() (or an agent's own resolveGate primitive) closes — a thin specialization of the existing 'human' WaitSignal with a durable, queryable gate token. (5) ctx.stream() emits partial progress frames over the existing event bus / signalStore, explicitly NON-durable (only the final step output is checkpointed). The engine supplies the durable loop/inject/wait/fan-out; the agent supplies all logic. This turns streamline into a DBOS/Restate-style checkpoint-resume substrate for dynamic agent graphs while preserving every existing durability invariant.
design.internalChanges:
GRAPH MODEL (the load-bearing change) — workflow/registry.ts: getNextStep() (registry.ts:42-48) and createRun() (registry.ts:53-84) currently read the immutable, version-pinned definition.steps array. Add a per-run overlay: a new optional run field graphOverlay?: InjectableStep[] (and the injected StepState entries already live in run.steps[]). getNextStep gains a run-aware sibling getNextStepForRun(run, currentStepId) that first walks run.steps order (which now includes injected steps) and falls back to definition order — definition.steps stays the static base graph, run.steps is the effective graph. executor.moveToNextStep (executor.ts:684-732) switches to the run-aware lookup. This keeps version-pinning intact: the BASE definition is never mutated, so a v1 run still resolves to v1's engine; injected steps are run-local data and survive resume because they are persisted in run.steps + graphOverlay.

INJECT — context.ts: new injectSteps() does a DB-write-first $push of new StepState entries onto run.steps (status:'pending') guarded by CANCELLED_GUARD + signal.aborted check, then mirrors in-memory. Handler resolution: executor.executeStep (executor.ts:219-220) getHandler falls through to a new dynamicHandlers map on the registry when the static handler is absent. StepNotFoundError (executor.ts:222-225) only throws if neither static nor dynamic handler resolves.

LOOP — context.ts: ctx.loop wraps the existing checkpoint/getCheckpoint slot (context.ts:245-274). State machine lives entirely in the handler; engine only persists {iter,state,done}. No executor change needed — loop runs inside a single step's handler invocation; heartbeats keep flowing via the auto-timer (executor.ts:412) and the loop should call ctx.heartbeat() each iteration (documented).

FAN-OUT — context.ts: spawnAgents composes ctx.startChildWorkflow's WaitSignal('childWorkflow') (context.ts:163-170) with scatter's checkpoint replay (context.ts:176-243). Engine handleWaitingState childWorkflow branch (engine.ts:906-1005) extends to track an ARRAY of childRunIds and a joinPolicy; the existing dual-bus completion listener pattern is reused per child. Cancel-propagation hooks engine.cancel (engine.ts:1428-1463 narrow-$set path) to read childRunIds from the step checkpoint and cancel each child.

GATE — context.ts ctx.gate throws WaitSignal('gate', reason, {gateId}) (new type literal, core/types.ts:126). executor.handleWait (executor.ts:495-516) already persists any WaitSignal generically — only the type union widens. engine.handleWaitingState (engine.ts:871-1009) adds a 'gate' branch that simply breaks (parks, zero compute) like 'human'. engine.resolveGate is a thin wrapper over resumeWaitingWorkflow (engine.ts:1226) that locates the run by gateId via a new indexed query and calls executor.resumeStep with the decision. A new STEP_MACHINE/RUN_MACHINE entry is NOT needed (gate reuses waiting<->running transitions already in status.ts:84-117).

STREAM — context.ts ctx.stream is a 3-line emit onto eventBus + signalStore (mirrors ctx.emit, context.ts:117-123) under new 'step:progress' event; added to EventPayloadMap (events.ts:51-71) and to createEventSink default list (events.ts:165-179).

checkNoProgress (engine.ts:846-865): injectSteps that appends after current and advances is normal progress (currentStepId changes). Loop-internal iteration does NOT change currentStepId and stays inside one handler call, so it never reaches checkNoProgress — no signal needed. Verified this is the #1 trap and it is avoided by design.

PUBLIC BARREL — index.ts: export new GateToken/InjectableStep types, resolveGate is reachable via createWorkflow's returned engine handle.
design.mongokitUsage:
Leverages mongokit where it is the right tool, hand-rolls where the map says it must:
- injectSteps $push and loop/fan-out checkpoints route through WorkflowRunRepository.updateOne -> mongokit findOneAndUpdate, inheriting the schema-level writeConcern {w:'majority',j:true} (run.model.ts:131). No per-call write concern needed (mongokit has none — confirmed in the capability map).
- resolveGate uses mongokit Repository.claim() (the same primitive resume() already uses, engine.ts:1195) to atomically flip the gate's waiting->running transition: claim(runId, {field:'status', from:'waiting', to:'running', where:{ 'steps.$[].waitingFor.data.gateId': gateId }}, patch). Null-on-miss = another worker/agent already resolved the gate — treated as race-loss, not error.
- spawnAgents join: reuse the existing childWorkflow event-bus completion listeners (engine.ts:956-988); NO change streams (mongokit has none — capability map §5). Durable join survives crash via the scatter-style checkpoint of childRunIds, recovered by the scheduler's existing childWorkflow resume path, not by a watch.
- New gate lookup index: add a partial compound index on steps.waitingFor.data.gateId (sparse, only on waiting runs) declared on the Mongoose schema (run.model.ts indexes block ~135-256), per the rule that mongokit does NOT create indexes — index DDL stays hand-rolled in the model/retention layer.
- No multi-doc transaction needed: every primitive mutates exactly one run document, so single-doc atomicity + claim CAS is sufficient (capability map §4: reach for batchTransaction only for cross-doc; not the case here).
design.durabilityModel:
Checkpoint-resume (DBOS/Restate model), NOT deterministic replay — matches the engine's existing model and the best-in-class checklist.
- injectSteps: DB-write-first $push of StepState (status:'pending') + graphOverlay, then in-memory mirror, under CANCELLED_GUARD; modifiedCount===0 means cancelled/race -> re-read, don't assume. Idempotent: dedupe by stepId so a crashed-mid-inject retry that re-runs the handler re-pushes only missing steps (use $addToSet-style guard on stepId, or check existing run.steps before $push).
- loop: each iteration's {iter,state,done} persisted via the existing checkpoint slot AFTER the agent's body() resolves. Crash before checkpoint -> iteration re-runs (at-least-once per iteration); crash after -> resume skips it. The agent's tool/LLM call inside body() is therefore effectively-once per iteration and the engine NEVER re-calls a completed iteration. Non-deterministic LLM output is captured in state and reused on resume — exactly the agentic requirement.
- spawnAgents: childRunIds checkpointed before parking; child completion is at-least-once (event bus), deduped by the existing _resumed flag (engine.ts:945,958). Parent resumes from the join checkpoint, re-collecting only not-yet-terminal children.
- gate: durable as a 'waiting' run document = zero idle compute. resolveGate via claim() is exactly-once on the transition (CAS); a double-resolve loses the race and returns current state.
- stream: explicitly NON-durable — only the step's final return value is checkpointed. Mid-stream frames are fire-and-forget over the bus; this is correct (mid-stream tokens should not be individually journaled, per the checklist).
- Idempotency-key start dedupe (run.model.ts:191-200) and version-pinning (definitionVersion write-once) are untouched; injected steps live in run.steps not the pinned definition, so migrateRun semantics are unaffected.
correctnessConcerns: Dynamic injection vs version pinning: injected steps MUST go into the per-run run.steps[]/graphOverlay, never the shared definition.steps (it is version-pinned and shared across all runs of that engine version). Mutating the definition would corrupt every concurrent run and break lookupVersion. The design enforces this by making getNextStepForRun read run.steps, leaving definition.steps as the immutable base.

checkNoProgress trap (engine.ts:846-865): an inject that appends 'after-current' and advances currentStepId is genuine progress and is detected normally; loop iterations never change currentStepId but run inside one handler call so they never reach checkNoProgress. Verified no goto-style 'skipped' signal is required — but any future inject mode that keeps currentStepId constant MUST teach checkNoProgress, same trap goto hit.

injectSteps idempotency: a crash between $push and handler completion can re-run the handler. Must dedupe by stepId (check run.steps / $addToSet guard) so re-injection is a no-op; otherwise duplicate steps execute.

Loop at-least-once per iteration: body() can run twice for the iteration that was in-flight at crash. The agent's tool call inside body() must be idempotent OR carry a caller-supplied idempotency key — engine cannot make an arbitrary external LLM/tool call exactly-once (honest BIC caveat). Document this; do not market loop as exactly-once.

Heartbeats during long loops: a multi-iteration loop is ONE step invocation; if an iteration is slow the auto-heartbeat timer (executor.ts:412) covers it, but a loop that blocks the event loop without awaiting will starve heartbeats and trip the HEARTBEAT_FAILURE_ABORT_THRESHOLD double-execution guard. Document ctx.heartbeat() per iteration.

spawnAgents cancel-propagation race: cancelling the parent must cancel children read from the checkpoint, but a child may complete concurrently with the cancel. Use claim-guarded cancel per child (null-on-miss = already terminal) and never spread the whole run on cancel (mongoose-9 subdoc cache poison, engine.ts:1451-1463).

Gate resolution race / cross-run resolve: ctx.resolveGate lets agent run A resolve run B's gate. resolveGate MUST use claim() CAS on waiting->running so two resolvers don't double-complete the step; the partial index on gateId must be unique-enough or the lookup must scope by runId+gateId to avoid resolving the wrong run.

Gate lookup needs an index: querying steps.waitingFor.data.gateId without a (sparse, partial-on-waiting) compound index full-scans workflow_runs — mongokit will warn but it must be declared on the schema.

Stream ordering / loss: 'step:progress' frames are at-most-once over the bus and may arrive out of order or be dropped (no replay). The host SSE layer owns ordering/replay; the engine must never let a dropped frame affect step outcome — enforced by keeping stream() side-effect-free on run state.

Injected handler resolution must stay a NAMED-HANDLER REGISTRY: handlerKey selects a pre-registered in-code handler in config.dynamicHandlers; if the engine ever eval'd/deserialized code from the injected data it would violate the hard rule. StepNotFoundError on an unknown handlerKey, never dynamic code execution.
design.philosophyNote: Fits the primitives-not-orchestrator rule, but only because each primitive was deliberately bounded to the engine's neutral half: the engine owns the DURABLE MECHANICS (inject into the per-run graph, persist loop checkpoints, park/resume on gates, fan-out/join children, fire progress events) and the agent owns ALL LOGIC (what to inject, the loop body, when to open/resolve a gate, what to stream). No LLM client, prompt, model, cost table, or business rule enters the engine. The one place this could slide into orchestrator territory is ctx.injectSteps + dynamicHandlers: it is kept legal by being a strict NAMED-HANDLER REGISTRY — the injected JSON only SELECTS pre-registered in-code handlers and wires their inputs; it never ships executable logic as data and the engine never deserializes the dead definition.model.ts condition string. This is exactly the line the hard rule draws. Two honest caveats: (a) loop and the tool-call iterator are effectively-once per iteration, not exactly-once against external APIs — must be documented, never marketed as exactly-once; (b) the per-run graph overlay is the riskiest change (it reinterprets the single graph seam getNextStep), so it must ship behind careful tests proving version-pinning and migrateRun are unaffected. Recommendation: ACCEPT, but split delivery — ship gate + stream + spawnAgents first (low blast radius, reuse existing wait/childWorkflow/event seams), then loop, then injectSteps last (it touches the core graph model). Cost accounting for any LLM/tool spend stays in the HOST (Prism pipelines/cost.ts) — the engine emits 'step:progress'/'step:completed' and the host meters; no cost hook in the engine.

verdict.area: Agentic-workflow primitives (injectSteps, durable loop, spawnAgents fan-out/join, agent-managed gates, streaming progress) for @classytic/streamline v2.3.4
correctnessHoles: FATAL — childWorkflow/spawnAgents have NO crash recovery; the design's central durability claim is false. Verified: CommonQueries.readyToResume (query-builder.ts:166) requires waitingFor.type='timer' AND resumeAt<=now; readyForRetry requires retryAfter; staleRunning (query-builder.ts:173) matches status='running' only. A parent parked on a child (status='waiting', waitingFor.type='childWorkflow') is selected by NONE of the scheduler poll classes (smart-scheduler.ts:473-476 only call getReadyToResume + getReadyForRetry). Completion is delivered ONLY by the in-process event listeners registered LIVE inside handleWaitingState (engine.ts:983-988). On worker crash those listeners die and nothing re-enters handleWaitingState for that run (no orphan/re-subscribe path exists anywhere in engine.ts — grep confirms childWorkflow appears only at 906/919/994). Result: the parent wedges forever. spawnAgents is built directly on this and the design explicitly states join 'survives crash via ... the scheduler's existing childWorkflow resume path' — that path does NOT exist. This is a pre-existing latent bug the proposal would promote into a load-bearing agentic primitive.

GRAPH-INDEX CORRUPTION — injectSteps breaks the definition-index/run.steps-index alignment that existing primitives silently depend on. Verified: claimStepExecution (executor.ts:263) and updateStepState (executor.ts:647) use run.steps.findIndex (live array) = injection-safe. BUT handleGoto (executor.ts:765) computes targetIndex = registry.definition.steps.findIndex(...) and then writes steps.${targetIndex}.status — once an injected step shifts run.steps relative to the immutable definition.steps, goto writes to the WRONG step slot. The design's getNextStepForRun must replace registry.getNextStep at moveToNextStep (executor.ts:685) AND every definition-index write site (goto), but the proposal only mentions moveToNextStep. Underspecified = corruption.

resolveGate CAS FILTER IS INVALID. Design proposes claim(runId,{from:'waiting',to:'running',where:{'steps.$[].waitingFor.data.gateId':gateId}},...). 'steps.$[]' is an array-update positional operator — illegal in a QUERY filter (mongokit claim AND-merges `where` into the find filter, Repository.ts:1508-1512). The matching multikey query path is 'steps.waitingFor.data.gateId'. As written the claim never matches → resolveGate is a silent no-op returning null (mis-read as race-loss).

GATE CROSS-RUN LOOKUP UNDERSPECIFIED + RACE. engine.resolveGate(gateId,decision) takes no runId, so it must locate the run by gateId. gateId must be globally unique AND the lookup must scope by runId to avoid resolving the wrong run; design hand-waves this ('unique-enough'). Also resumeWaitingWorkflow (engine.ts:1226-1242) only resumes run.currentStepId — gate resolution only works if the gate step IS currentStepId; if any injected/loop step advanced currentStepId past the gate, resolveGate targets the wrong step.

LOOP heartbeat starvation is real and under-mitigated. A multi-iteration loop is ONE step invocation; if an iteration's body() blocks without awaiting, the auto-heartbeat setInterval (executor.ts:412) is starved → consecutiveHeartbeatFailures crosses HEARTBEAT_FAILURE_ABORT_THRESHOLD → step self-aborts (executor.ts:440-446) AND the stale detector re-claims on another worker = double-execution of the in-flight iteration. 'Document ctx.heartbeat() per iteration' is a guideline, not an enforced invariant.

spawnAgents cancel-propagation race: cancel() sends only narrow $set (engine.ts:1458-1463) and does NOT currently read childRunIds. The design hooks cancel to walk checkpointed childRunIds and cancel each — but a child completing concurrently with the parent-cancel can re-emit workflow:completed to a listener that's mid-teardown; must use claim-guarded per-child cancel (null=already terminal) AND must not resurrect the cancelled parent (CANCELLED_GUARD on the resume path).

deriveRunStatus (status.ts:53-64) has no notion of partial fan-out. joinPolicy:'first' / 'allSettled' implies the parent should proceed while some children are non-terminal — but the parent is a single 'waiting' step; there is no per-child step status. Partial-join semantics live entirely in the handler's checkpoint, so a crash mid-join re-collects via at-least-once child completion — acceptable ONLY if the child resume path exists (see fatal #1).

injectSteps idempotency hole on crash-between-$push-and-handler-return: the design's dedupe-by-stepId is correct in principle, but $addToSet does not work on an array of StepState objects keyed by a sub-field; you must pre-check run.steps for existing stepId then $push, which is itself a TOCTOU under concurrent workers. Needs a single atomic update with a negative filter ({'steps.stepId': {$ne: id}}) per injected step, not $addToSet.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: PASS, with one watch-item. The engine half (durable inject mechanics, checkpoint persistence, park/resume, fan-out, event emit) vs agent half (what to inject, loop body, gate decisions, stream frames) split is respected. Critically verified: definition.model.ts:30/60 ships a `condition: String` serialized field that is dead storage and never deserialized — the design correctly does NOT revive it, and dynamicHandlers is a strict named-handler registry (handlerKey SELECTS a pre-registered in-code StepHandler, never eval'd). No LLM client, prompt, model, cost table, or domain rule enters the engine; cost stays in Prism's host. The only smell: ctx.injectSteps + dynamicHandlers is exactly the line — it stays legal only as long as handlerKey resolution throws StepNotFoundError on unknown keys and the engine never reads the dead condition string. Keep a guard test asserting dynamicHandlers cannot carry serialized code.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: MIXED. Correct: routing inject/loop/fan-out writes through repository.updateOne→findOneAndUpdate inherits schema-level writeConcern {w:'majority',j:true} (run.model.ts:131) — mongokit has no per-call writeConcern, confirmed, so this is the right call. Single-doc atomicity is sufficient (no batchTransaction needed). WRONG: the resolveGate claim() uses an array-update positional path ('steps.$[]') inside the CAS `where` filter, which mongokit AND-merges as a query predicate (Repository.ts:1508-1512) — it must be the multikey query path 'steps.waitingFor.data.gateId' or an $elemMatch. Also: claim()'s from===to no-op write-skip (Repository.ts:1524-1543) is irrelevant here (waiting→running differ) — fine. The design ignores a genuinely better mongokit primitive for the childWorkflow/gate park-resume durability gap: leasePlugin / createMongoLockAdapter do NOT solve it (the gap is a missing poll query, not a lock), but the real fix is a new repository query (CommonQueries.readyChildWorkflows / readyGates) + a scheduler poll branch routed through the existing queryLean/claim path — idiomatic and consistent with §4/§6 of the capability map.
verdict.breakingVerdict: The 'minor-additive' label is HALF-honest. New ctx.* methods, optional WorkflowConfig.dynamicHandlers, a new EventPayloadMap key, and a new WaitingFor.type literal ('gate') are genuinely additive. BUT: (1) replacing registry.getNextStep with a run-aware getNextStepForRun changes THE graph-resolution seam every existing run flows through — if goto's definition-index writes (executor.ts:765) aren't migrated in lockstep, existing goto-using workflows silently corrupt = a hidden breaking change masquerading as additive. (2) Adding 'gate' to WaitingFor.type is type-additive but the new sparse gate index (run.model.ts indexes block) requires a deploy-time index build with the same pre-deploy duplicate-cleanup caveat class as the idempotency index (run.model.ts:158-189) — must be called out as an operational migration, not silent. (3) Fixing the childWorkflow recovery gap (required for spawnAgents to be honest) is itself a behavioral change to crash semantics of an existing primitive — semver-minor only because it fixes a bug, but must ship with a migration note for operators who built around the (broken) current behavior.
requiredFixes: BLOCKER: Add a durable resume path for childWorkflow/gate waits BEFORE shipping spawnAgents. Implement CommonQueries.readyChildWorkflows (status='waiting', waitingFor.type in ['childWorkflow','gate'], notPaused) backed by a new compound index, a getReadyChildWorkflows repo query, and a scheduler poll branch that on recovery re-enters handleWaitingState (re-subscribes the completion listener and reconciles child terminal status by polling childRunIds). Without this, NEITHER childWorkflow nor spawnAgents nor gate is crash-durable and the design's core claim is false.

Fix resolveGate CAS: use where:{'steps.waitingFor.data.gateId': gateId} (multikey) or $elemMatch, NOT 'steps.$[]'. Scope the gate lookup by a globally-unique gateId AND assert the resolved step is the run's waiting step. Add the proposed sparse/partial gate index and document its deploy-time build as an operational migration.

Specify the FULL graph-model migration, not just moveToNextStep: every site that derives a step array index from registry.definition.steps must switch to a run-aware index against run.steps — specifically handleGoto's targetIndex (executor.ts:765) and rewindRun (registry.ts:90). Add a regression test proving goto + injectSteps interaction writes the correct step slot, and that version-pinning/migrateRun are unaffected (a v1 run with injected steps still resolves to v1's base graph).

injectSteps idempotency: inject each step via a single atomic updateOne with filter {_id, CANCELLED_GUARD, 'steps.stepId': {$ne: stepId}} + $push, so a crash-retry that re-runs the handler cannot duplicate-push. Do not use $addToSet on StepState objects.

Enforce loop heartbeats rather than documenting them: have ctx.loop() call ctx.heartbeat() automatically after each iteration's checkpoint, so a CPU-bound iteration cannot trip the HEARTBEAT_FAILURE_ABORT_THRESHOLD double-execution guard.

spawnAgents cancel-propagation: read childRunIds from the checkpoint and cancel each via claim-guarded cancel (null=already terminal=ok); keep cancel's narrow-$set pattern (engine.ts:1458) and never resurrect a cancelled parent. Add a test for child-completes-during-parent-cancel.

Documentation/marketing: loop and the tool-call iterator are EFFECTIVELY-ONCE per iteration (at-least-once on the crash-in-flight iteration), never exactly-once against external LLM/tool APIs — require caller idempotency keys. ctx.stream frames are at-most-once, unordered, no replay — host owns SSE ordering; engine must keep stream() side-effect-free on run state.

Add a philosophy guard test: dynamicHandlers entries are in-code StepHandlers only; assert the engine never reads definition.model.ts's serialized `condition` string and throws StepNotFoundError on unknown handlerKey.

design.area: Large-scale distributed execution — worker leasing, partitioned claiming, push-wake, backpressure (mongokit-backed)
design.summary: Streamline today is already multi-worker-safe for the *resume/recover* path: every transition goes through an atomic `claim()` CAS on the `running` status, and stale-recovery is gated by a heartbeat-age `where:` guard (`engine.ts:1264` recoverStale, `run.repository.ts:352` markStaleAsFailed). What it lacks for *large-scale* distribution is (1) explicit worker ownership — there is no `ownedBy`/`workerId` field, recovery is coarse heartbeat-age only; (2) claim-side partitioning — every worker scans the same `{status, updatedAt}` indexes, so a hot "next runnable" set contends; (3) push-based wake — the scheduler is poll-only. This upgrade adds three additive, opt-in primitives that fix exactly those gaps without turning the engine into an orchestrator: a worker-lease ownership stamp layered on the existing heartbeat model via mongokit's `leasePlugin`, a deterministic run-partition field (`partitionKey`) plus partition-scoped scheduler queries so N schedulers shard the claim set with no central coordinator, and an optional change-stream "wake source" the host can plug in to collapse poll latency (mongokit has no change-stream abstraction, so this is a host-supplied adapter the engine merely subscribes to). All of it keeps the schema-level `{w:'majority',j:true}` and CAS-or-null semantics untouched. It makes streamline horizontally scalable to tens of thousands of concurrent runs with per-partition (not global) ordering and effectively-once dispatch — the honest Mongo ceiling.
design.internalChanges:
Five real seams, all additive:

1. `storage/run.model.ts` — add three OPTIONAL fields beside `lastHeartbeat` (line 93): `ownedBy?: String`, `leaseExpiresAt?: Date`, `partitionKey?: Number`. Add two compound indexes mirroring the existing scheduler ones but partition-prefixed: `{ partitionKey:1, status:1, lastHeartbeat:1 }` and `{ partitionKey:1, status:1, paused:1, steps:1, updatedAt:1, _id:1 }`. Schema-level write concern untouched. Per cross-cutting invariant #9, these are NEW fields, not reuse of `output`.

2. `workflow/registry.ts:53` createRun — stamp `partitionKey = hash(runId) % partitions.count` at create-time (write-once, sibling to the existing `definitionVersion` pin at line 80). Stable across versions, so version-pinning invariant holds.

3. `storage/query-builder.ts` CommonQueries — add a `.partitions([...])` clause appended to `readyToResume`/`readyForRetry`/`staleRunning`/`scheduledReady` as `{ partitionKey: { $in: owned } }` when partitions configured; no-op otherwise. The repository verbs (`run.repository.ts:271-457`) thread `opts.partitions` into these.

4. `execution/engine.ts:1264` recoverStale + `executor.ts:412` heartbeat timer — the heartbeat `updateOne` additionally `$set`s `leaseExpiresAt = now + leaseTtlMs` and `ownedBy = workerId` (renewal). recoverStale's `where:` guard changes from heartbeat-age-only to `$or:[{leaseExpiresAt:{$lt:now}},{leaseExpiresAt:{$exists:false}}]` — precise lease expiry instead of coarse heartbeat age. The existing heartbeat double-execution abort (`executor.ts:440`) is unchanged and remains the local-side guard.

5. `execution/smart-scheduler.ts:449` poll + `engine.ts:377` callback wiring — poll passes `this.partitions.owned` into the repo reads; a new `WakeSource` subscription (wired in container alongside `setStaleRecoveryCallback`) calls a debounced `poll()` (or a targeted single-run resume when `runId` hint present). Poll stays as the durability backstop — push only reduces latency, never the source of truth. `checkNoProgress` (engine.ts:846) is untouched (no new control-flow verb, currentStepId semantics unchanged).
design.mongokitUsage:
Heavy, deepening existing adoption — no hand-rolled Mongo added:

- **`leasePlugin` (`mongokit/src/plugins/lease.plugin.ts`)** composed into `WorkflowRunRepository` constructor (`run.repository.ts:117`), configured with `leasedByField:'ownedBy'`, `leaseExpiresAtField:'leaseExpiresAt'`, `statusField:'status'`. `methodRegistryPlugin()` (its prerequisite) is ALREADY composed there. `claimWithLease`/`renewLease`/`releaseLease` delegate to the plugin's `lease`/`extend`/`release`, so tenant/audit/cache hooks fire — the manual `bypassTenant` merge for `_id`-scoped writes stays (the known `before:claim` write-filter gap from the mongokit map §2).
- **`claim()` / CAS-or-null (`Repository.ts:1477`)** remains the transition primitive; lease stamping rides as extra `$set` keys in the same atomic update — no second write, durability envelope preserved.
- **`cursor()` keyset streaming** already used by `cursorStaleRunning`; partition variant just adds `{partitionKey:{$in}}` to the filter — the partition-prefixed compound index keeps it a bounded scan (mongokit keyset index-compat warning honored).
- **Change streams: mongokit has NONE** (grep-confirmed across `mongokit/src`). So the engine does NOT open a change stream itself; the `WakeSource` interface lets the HOST bridge one (or Redis/Kafka) — matching the mongokit map's "change-stream triggering stays hand-rolled / pull-model" verdict. Clock-skew caveat from leasePlugin (client-side `new Date()`) applies: lease TTL must exceed max NTP skew; documented in the option JSDoc.
design.durabilityModel:
Unchanged foundations: schema-level `{w:'majority',j:true}` (`run.model.ts:131`), DB-write-first-then-mirror, CANCELLED_GUARD on every executor write, CAS-or-null on every transition. New guarantees layered on top:

- **Crash-durable ownership:** `ownedBy` + `leaseExpiresAt` are stamped inside the SAME atomic `claim()` that transitions the run to `running` (`claimWithLease`) — one journaled majority write, no window where a run is running-but-unowned. The existing heartbeat timer (`executor.ts:412`) renews `leaseExpiresAt` on the cadence it already writes `lastHeartbeat`, so a live worker holds its lease; a crashed worker's lease simply expires.
- **Idempotent recovery:** recoverStale's CAS now keys on `leaseExpiresAt < now` (precise) instead of heartbeat age (coarse). Reset of the in-flight step `running→pending` (engine.ts:1300) and `recoveryAttempts` `$inc` → dead-letter bound are all preserved, so the recovery path stays idempotent and bounded (`run.model.ts:117`).
- **`partitionKey` write-once at createRun**, same contract as `definitionVersion` — recovery and resume always resolve the same partition, no migration drift.
- **Push-wake is advisory, never authoritative:** a missed/duplicate wake event cannot corrupt state because the poll backstop + atomic claim are the real source of truth. WakeSource at-least-once delivery is fine; the claim CAS dedupes. This is "effectively-once dispatch," explicitly NOT bare exactly-once.
correctnessConcerns: Lease vs heartbeat double-write: leaseExpiresAt and lastHeartbeat must be set in the SAME updateOne the heartbeat timer already issues (executor.ts:412), not a second write, or a crash between them leaves a renewed heartbeat with a stale lease and recovery never fires.

Clock skew (mongokit leasePlugin uses client-side new Date()): a forward-drifted worker can steal an unexpired lease early → double execution. Mitigation: leaseTtlMs must be >> max fleet NTP skew AND the executor.ts:440 local heartbeat-abort still fires, so the original owner aborts before the thief commits. Document NTP requirement.

Partition rebalance: if `partitions.owned` changes on redeploy (e.g. scaling 4→8 schedulers), runs in now-unowned partitions must not be orphaned. The poll backstop on ANY scheduler whose owned set covers that partition picks them up; require that the union of all owned sets always == [0..count). A startup assertion + a 'full-sweep fallback' mode (owned=undefined polls all partitions) covers transient gaps.

Hot-doc contention NOT fully solved: a single 'highest-priority next run' still contends if all partitions funnel to it. Partitioning reduces but cannot eliminate; global strict-FIFO across partitions is impossible on this model (per-partition ordering only). Must be stated, not promised away.

WakeSource feedback storm: a change stream that fires onWake per run write could thundering-herd the scheduler. Debounce/coalesce wakes (single pending poll) and treat runId hints as targeted single-run resume to avoid full polls.

recoverStale changing its where-guard from heartbeat-age to leaseExpiresAt is a behaviour change for runs written by OLD workers that never set leaseExpiresAt — the `$or:[{leaseExpiresAt:{$exists:false}}]` branch must remain so mixed-version fleets during rollout still recover legacy runs.
Tenant scope: partition reads are cross-tenant sweeps (bypassTenant:true) like the existing ones; per-row lease writes stay _id-scoped. Preserve the split or strict-tenant mode throws.
design.philosophyNote: FITS — and was deliberately bounded to fit. Worker leasing, run partitioning, and lease-precise recovery are pure execution-substrate primitives: they decide WHICH worker runs a step and WHEN it is safe to reclaim it, never WHAT the step does. No business logic, prompts, cost tables, or domain rules enter the engine. The hard rule's danger zone here is the WakeSource and the scheduler: I explicitly kept orchestration OUT by (a) making the engine merely *subscribe* to a host-supplied WakeSource rather than opening/owning a change stream or message bus, and (b) keeping poll as the authoritative durability path so push is a latency optimization, not a control plane. Cost accounting stays in Prism's pipelines/cost.ts — untouched. The one thing I rejected from the 'best-in-class' checklist as out-of-engine: a built-in matching-service / global-FIFO / sub-second push dispatcher. That would require a dedicated substrate (Temporal-style sharded log) Mongo doesn't provide, and building it would drag fairness/priority *policy* into the engine. Honest ceiling stated in correctnessConcerns: per-partition ordering, effectively-once (not exactly-once), poll-augmented-by-push (not push-native) latency. Everything proposed is additive, opt-in (defaults reproduce v2.3.4), and crash-durable/idempotent by riding the existing claim()+heartbeat+writeConcern machinery.

verdict.area: Large-scale distributed execution — worker leasing, partitioned claiming, push-wake, backpressure (mongokit-backed)
correctnessHoles: Architecture mismatch defeats the lease premise: recovery is scheduler-PUSH, not worker-PULL. `engine.recoverStale` ends with `return this.execute(runId)` (engine.ts:1320), running the recovered run INLINE on whichever scheduler polled it (smart-scheduler.ts:567-569). No fleet of workers competes to claim a run by id and run it. So `ownedBy`/`workerId` records which scheduler-process picked it up, but adds no new safety the heartbeat-age CAS at engine.ts:1281 didn't already provide. Worker leasing answers 'which worker runs a step' — a question this engine's dispatch model doesn't ask.

Split-brain staleness clocks. The design changes ONLY recoverStale's where-guard to `leaseExpiresAt < now` (engine.ts:1281) but leaves `markStaleAsFailed` (run.repository.ts:367) and `markAsDeadLettered` (run.repository.ts:410) keyed on `lastHeartbeat`/`recoveryAttempts`. Now recovery-eligibility (lease clock) and dead-letter-eligibility (heartbeat clock) diverge. With leaseTtlMs = 2x staleHeartbeatThresholdMs, a run becomes recover-eligible at a DIFFERENT time than it becomes terminate-eligible, so the bounded-recovery → dead-letter backstop (run.model.ts:117) can be bypassed or fire prematurely. Either all paths move to the lease clock or none do — you cannot change one in isolation.

Dual-write crash window the design itself flags but does not eliminate. The heartbeat timer (executor.ts:414) currently $sets ONLY lastHeartbeat. The design adds leaseExpiresAt + ownedBy renewal to that same write — fine — but the very FIRST stamping happens in claimWithLease at run-start, and the heartbeat interval (TIMING.HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_MS) is the renewal cadence. If leaseTtlMs is not provably > heartbeat interval + max single-step time, a long legitimate step lets the lease lapse and recoverStale fires a SECOND inline execution on another scheduler while the first is still mid-step. The existing heartbeat-failure abort (executor.ts:440) only fires on DB WRITE failure, NOT on a lease-stolen-while-writes-succeed condition — so the local double-exec guard does NOT cover the new failure mode.

Clock-skew regression vs current model. Today's recovery compares lastHeartbeat (a timestamp the worker wrote and Mongo majority-acked) against the sweeper's own `now`. The new predicate compares a worker-authored `leaseExpiresAt` (worker's `new Date() + ttl`) against another machine's `now`. A forward-drifted scheduler can judge an unexpired lease expired and re-dispatch. mongokit's leasePlugin (lease.plugin.ts:164,204,241) is entirely client-`new Date()` based, so adopting it INTRODUCES skew sensitivity the heartbeat-age path was less exposed to. 'Require NTP within leaseMs' is a real operational constraint that must be a hard precondition, not a JSDoc footnote.

Partition rebalance orphaning has no enforcement. The 'union of owned sets == [0..count)' invariant is asserted in prose only. A redeploy that miscounts (scaling 4→8 schedulers, a crashed instance owning a partition) silently strands every run in an unowned partition until a human notices. The proposed 'full-sweep fallback (owned=undefined)' reintroduces the all-schedulers-scan-everything contention the partitioning was supposed to remove — so the failure mode degrades to the pre-upgrade behavior at best, total stall at worst.

partitionKey = hash(runId) % count makes `count` effectively immutable. Changing partition count after runs exist re-maps existing runs' hash buckets; a run created when count=8 with partitionKey=5 is invisible to a fleet later reconfigured to count=4 owning [0,1,2,3]. This is the same fixed-shard-count constraint Temporal documents, but the design presents `partitions.count` as fleet config without stating it is write-once-per-deployment-era and cannot shrink without a migration sweep.

WakeSource targeted single-run resume bypasses concurrency gating. The poll path gates on countRunning vs maxConcurrentExecutions (smart-scheduler.ts:459-468) before dispatching. A WakeSource onWake(runId) that triggers a 'targeted single-run resume' (per internalChanges item 5) skips that admission check, so a wake storm can push concurrent executions past the configured global cap — the push path must route through the SAME slot accounting as poll, or it is a backpressure hole.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: FITS the primitives-not-orchestrator rule — this is the strongest part of the proposal. Worker identity, partition stamping, and lease-precise recovery are pure execution-substrate concerns: they decide which process runs a step and when reclaim is safe, never what a step does. No business logic, prompts, cost tables, or domain rules leak in; cost accounting correctly stays in Prism's pipelines/cost.ts. The one real danger zone — WakeSource — is correctly bounded: the engine SUBSCRIBES to a host-supplied adapter rather than owning a change stream or message bus, and poll stays authoritative so push is a latency optimization, not a control plane. Caveat: the 'targeted single-run resume on runId hint' edges toward the engine acting on external routing decisions; keep it strictly advisory (a hint that triggers a normal gated poll), never a privileged dispatch path, or it starts to smell like an orchestration seam.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: FORCED and partly fictional. The headline claim — claimWithLease/renewLease/releaseLease 'delegate to leasePlugin lease/extend/release' — does not hold. leasePlugin.lease() (lease.plugin.ts:146-191) takes NO id: it is a predicate-FIFO claim matching `{status: pendingStatus} OR {leaseExpiresAt < now}`, picks a sort winner, and flips status to `processingStatus`. streamline needs an id-TARGETED claim of a specific run with a state-machine transition (running→running for recovery). Those are different operations; claimWithLease cannot route through plugin.lease(). What it actually is: repo.claim(runId, transition, {$set:{ownedBy, leaseExpiresAt}}) — the EXISTING claim() primitive (Repository.ts:1477) with two extra $set keys, which IS the correct and idiomatic tool. So the lease plugin is never invoked. Worse, release()/extend() CAS on `leaseExpiresAt > now` and overwrite `status` to a terminal doneStatus (lease.plugin.ts:248-259) — that would corrupt streamline's `running` status and violate STEP_MACHINE/RUN_MACHINE (status.ts:84-117). Correct mongokit story: drop leasePlugin entirely; use claim() with lease fields as extra $set (durability + null-on-miss preserved, write-concern still rides the schema), use cursor() for partition-scoped streaming (already adopted), and keep the manual bypassTenant merge (the known before:claim write-filter gap). The mongokit map even hints this; the design over-reached by naming the plugin.
verdict.breakingVerdict: 'minor-additive' is ACCURATE for the public API and schema (three optional fields, opt-in options, partition queries no-op when unset — defaults reproduce v2.3.4). BUT one internal change is a silent behavior change for ALL users, not just opt-in ones: flipping recoverStale's where-guard from lastHeartbeat-age to leaseExpiresAt (internalChanges item 4) alters recovery semantics for runs that never set leaseExpiresAt. The design keeps a `{leaseExpiresAt: {$exists:false}}` branch for legacy runs, which preserves recoverability — but it changes the recovery TIMING/condition for the default (non-partitioned, no-workerId) configuration too, because the heartbeat timer would now also start stamping leaseExpiresAt the moment the code ships. That makes the staleness-clock divergence (see correctness holes) a change that affects users who never opted in. Fix: gate ALL lease-clock behavior behind 'distributed options configured'; when unconfigured, recoverStale and the heartbeat write must stay byte-for-byte lastHeartbeat-only. With that gate, minor-additive is honest. Without it, it is a behavioral semver-minor risk that needs an explicit migration note.
requiredFixes: Drop the leasePlugin claim. Implement claimWithLease as repo.claim(runId, transition, {$set:{ownedBy, leaseExpiresAt, ...patch}}, {bypassTenant:true}) — the existing primitive with extra $set keys. Implement renewLease/releaseLease as plain CAS via claim/updateOne with a `where:{ownedBy: workerId}` guard. Do NOT use leasePlugin.lease/extend/release: wrong shape (predicate-FIFO, no id) and its terminal-status flip violates RUN_MACHINE.

Resolve the split-brain clock: pick ONE staleness clock for the configured-distributed mode. If recovery moves to leaseExpiresAt, markStaleAsFailed, markAsDeadLettered, and the retention sweeper must ALL key on leaseExpiresAt (with the recoveryAttempts $inc and dead-letter bound preserved). Add a test asserting recover-eligibility and terminate-eligibility derive from the same field.

Gate every behavior change behind 'distributed options configured'. When workerId/partitions/leaseTtlMs are all unset, recoverStale's where-guard and the heartbeat updateOne must remain lastHeartbeat-only, byte-for-byte v2.3.4. This keeps the minor-additive claim honest for the default install.

Make leaseTtlMs a validated invariant, not a default: assert leaseTtlMs > HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_MS + maxExpectedStepDurationMs at construction, and document NTP-within-leaseTtlMs as a hard precondition. Keep executor.ts:440 heartbeat-abort, but ALSO add a lease-loss check on the worker side (the renewing heartbeat already CAS-renews; if its renew CAS returns modifiedCount 0 / null because ownedBy no longer matches, abort the local step) — this closes the 'lease stolen while writes succeed' double-exec window the current abort does not cover.

Enforce the partition-coverage invariant in code: startup assertion that configured owned-sets across the fleet cover [0..count), plus a documented, bounded 'full-sweep fallback' that is explicitly a degraded mode (logs a warning, accepts contention). State that partitions.count is write-once per deployment era and shrinking it requires a re-stamp migration of existing runs.

Route WakeSource-triggered resumes through the SAME concurrency/admission gate as poll (countRunning vs maxConcurrentExecutions) and through the same atomic claim — never a privileged single-run dispatch path. Debounce/coalesce wakes into a single pending gated poll. Treat runId hints as advisory only.

Add the partition-prefixed compound indexes (internalChanges item 1) AND a test asserting the keyset cursor uses them (honor mongokit's index-compat warning at PaginationEngine), or partition-scoped cursorStaleRunning full-scans under load — the opposite of the goal.

design.summary: This "refactor" is ~85% already shipped in v2.3.4 and the remaining work is small and additive. run.repository.ts and concurrency-counter.repository.ts already extend mongokit's Repository, compose methodRegistryPlugin/mongoOperationsPlugin/tenantFilterPlugin, route run-level transitions through claim() (markStaleAsFailed, markAsDeadLettered), and use repo-core keyset/offset pagination; write concern is correctly pinned at the Mongoose schema level so every mongokit findOneAndUpdate inherits {w:'majority',j:true}. The genuine remaining gaps are three: (1) the general per-step transition path still uses guarded updateOne (findOneAndUpdate) rather than claim() — and it MUST stay that way because step state lives in the steps[] embedded array, where mongokit's single-stateField claim() does not apply; (2) definition.model.ts is the one true outlier still on raw mongoose.model + a plain-object repo + a pre('save') hook, never migrated to mongokit; (3) retention.ts TTL/partial-index DDL has no mongokit equivalent and must stay hand-rolled. The only net-new upgrades worth doing are migrating WorkflowDefinitionModel onto a real Repository, optionally moving the retention stale-sweep singleton guard onto createMongoLockAdapter, and adding a schema-level write-concern regression test. None of this touches engine orchestration semantics, so it is a clean semver-minor (additive) change.
design.publicApi: Additive only. New: `class WorkflowDefinitionRepository extends Repository<WorkflowDefinitionDoc>` exported from index.ts barrel (the existing plain-object `workflowDefinitionRepository` stays as a deprecated re-export delegating to a default instance, so no break). New optional config on createContainer: `retention.singletonLock?: LockAdapter` (from @classytic/repo-core) — when provided, StaleRunSweeper.sweepOnce wraps its scan in `lock.tryAcquire('streamline:stale-sweep', workerId, intervalMs)` so only one node sweeps; absent => current every-node behavior (unchanged default). New test-only export: none — instead ship an internal assertion `assertWriteConcern(WorkflowRunModel)` checking `schema.options.writeConcern?.w === 'majority' && j === true`, exercised by a package test. concurrency-counter public API unchanged (claimSlot/releaseSlot/reconcile stay). No signature changes to WorkflowRunRepository, StepContext, createWorkflow, or any ctx.* primitive.
design.internalChanges: storage/definition.model.ts: replace the bespoke `workflowDefinitionRepository` plain object and `pre('save')` semver hook with `WorkflowDefinitionRepository extends Repository<WorkflowDefinitionDoc>` (apply timestampPlugin to drop the manual createdAt/updatedAt, custom-id not needed). Keep semver-parse logic in a `beforeCreate`/`beforeUpdate` repo hook (mongokit before:create / before:update) instead of the mongoose pre-save, since mongokit's findOneAndUpdate path bypasses pre('save'). getLatestVersion/getActiveDefinitions become repo.getAll/aggregate calls; keep the unique {workflowId,version} index in the schema. storage/retention.ts: StaleRunSweeper.sweepOnce (retention.ts:246-371) gains an optional lock-guard wrapper; ensureIndex / syncRetentionIndexes / TTL specs are untouched (no mongokit equivalent — this is collection DDL). storage/run.repository.ts: NO change to updateOne/claim split — the per-step embedded-array writes correctly stay on guarded updateOne; only add a doc note that claim() is run-level-only. index.ts barrel: export the new class. No change to engine.ts, executor.ts, context.ts, status.ts, registry.ts, smart-scheduler.ts.
design.mongokitUsage: Already adopted and deepened, not introduced. Run transitions: Repository.claim()/findOneAndUpdate() (run.repository.ts markStaleAsFailed/markAsDeadLettered/updateOne) — claim() gives null-on-miss CAS with the from===to write-skip fast-path. Plugins composed at construction: methodRegistryPlugin (required first), mongoOperationsPlugin, tenantFilterPlugin. Pagination: repo-core OffsetPaginationResult/KeysetPaginationResult via getAll + cursor() streaming (cursorStaleRunning). Concurrency: deliberately NOT mongokit's incrementIfBelow — it lacks upsert:true + $setOnInsert + E11000-bootstrap that claimSlot needs, so the hand-rolled findOneAndUpdate stays (correct call). New: WorkflowDefinitionRepository extends Repository + timestampPlugin; optional createMongoLockAdapter (repo-core LockAdapter) for the sweep singleton. Explicitly N/A: mongokit has no change-stream abstraction and no index/TTL builder — retention.ts DDL and any change-stream triggering stay hand-rolled. Per-call writeConcern is NOT a mongokit feature; it stays on the schema and flows through every mongokit Model.findOneAndUpdate automatically.
design.durabilityModel: Unchanged and explicitly preserved. writeConcern {w:'majority',j:true} stays on WorkflowRunSchema (run.model.ts:131) and concurrency-counter.model.ts:62 — every mongokit method bottoms out at Model.findOneAndUpdate which inherits the schema-level concern, so no mongokit call weakens durability. The definition model migration adds the same schema-level concern guard if definitions become recovery-relevant (they are not today — they are config, not run state, so default concern is acceptable there). Idempotency: the partial-unique idempotencyKey index + E11000-catch in create() is untouched. State transitions remain DB-write-first then in-memory mirror with modifiedCount/null checks; claim()'s match-or-null is identical CAS semantics to the prior hand-rolled findOneAndUpdate precondition. The new singleton lock uses createMongoLockAdapter's E11000-race-safe tryAcquire with its own TTL index; losing the lock just means another node sweeps — no correctness loss if the lock is skipped.
correctnessConcerns: mongokit before:create/before:update hooks bypass mongoose pre('save') — the definition semver-parse MUST move into the repo hook or versionMajor/Minor/Patch silently stop populating on updates done via findOneAndUpdate.

Step-level writes live in steps[] embedded array; mongokit claim() keys on a single top-level stateField and CANNOT express the steps.$.status CAS — do NOT 'upgrade' executor.claimStepExecution to claim(); the guarded updateOne with CANCELLED_GUARD is the correct primitive and must stay.

Schema-level write concern is invisible to mongokit (it has no per-call writeConcern) — if anyone later moves it off the schema expecting mongokit to carry it, durability silently weakens. The assertWriteConcern test is the tripwire.

createMongoLockAdapter uses client-side new Date() for lease expiry — a forward-skewed clock can let a second node steal the sweep lock early, causing two concurrent sweeps. Sweep ops are themselves CAS-guarded (markStaleAsFailed claim) so this is a wasted-scan, not a corruption, but require NTP within leaseMs.

tenant-filter plugin still does not hook before:findOneAndUpdate write-filter merge — the manual applyTenantFilter in updateOne must NOT be deleted on the assumption the plugin now covers claim/findOneAndUpdate writes.
incrementIfBelow has no upsert path — replacing claimSlot with it would break first-call counter bootstrap and silently fail to cap concurrency on a cold bucket.
design.philosophyNote: Fits cleanly — this is pure storage-substrate plumbing, not orchestration. It adds no business logic, prompts, cost tables, or domain rules to the engine; it swaps hand-rolled Mongoose for mongokit repos that already back the engine, keeps cost accounting in the host, and ships zero executable-logic-as-data. One sharp caveat tied directly to the HARD RULE: definition.model.ts carries a `condition: String` 'serialized condition function' field and the audit flags it as dead storage that nothing hydrates. When migrating WorkflowDefinitionModel onto mongokit, DO NOT also wire a deserializer that turns that string into runnable code — that would cross the line from a named-handler registry into shipping executable logic as data and violate the primitives-not-orchestrator rule. Migrate it as inert metadata only (versioning/audit), exactly as it exists today, or drop the field. With that boundary held, the change is a legitimate, additive, durability-preserving deepening of an adoption already 85% complete.

correctnessHoles: SINGLETON-LOCK: no release path specified. The design wraps StaleRunSweeper.sweepOnce's scan in lock.tryAcquire('streamline:stale-sweep', workerId, intervalMs) but never calls lock.release in a finally. Combined with lease=intervalMs, a node that finishes a sweep early holds the lock until expiry, so NO node sweeps for the remainder of the interval — including the holder's own next scheduleNext tick (StaleRunSweeper self-reschedules every staleRunSweepIntervalMs at retention.ts:340-347). Net effect: sweep cadence is halved/jittered, stale runs sit longer before dead-lettering. Add release in finally OR use a lease shorter than the sweep period and accept best-effort.

LEASE DURATION WRONG: lease must exceed the max sweep duration, not equal the poll interval. getStaleRunningWorkflows batches up to staleRunBatchSize and each row does a markStaleAsFailed/markAsDeadLettered CAS round-trip (retention.ts:286-320). If that batch takes longer than intervalMs, the lease expires mid-sweep, a second node's tryAcquire matches the expired-lease $or branch (lock/index.ts:123), and two sweeps run concurrently. Design hand-waves this as 'wasted scan' — true that markStaleAsFailed/markAsDeadLettered are claim()-CAS-guarded so no double dead-letter — but recoveryAttempts $inc can be double-counted across the two sweeps if both read attempts=N before either writes, prematurely tripping maxStaleRecoveries -> dead-lettering a run that had one real recovery left. That IS a correctness regression vs today's single-sweeper behavior, not just wasted work.

CLOCK-SKEW introduces a NEW failure mode vs status quo. Today every node sweeps, guarded by per-row CAS — clock skew is irrelevant. The lock adapter uses client-side new Date() for both expiresAt and the $lt expiry predicate (lock/index.ts:111-123, documented at :50-61). A forward-skewed replica steals an unexpired lease early -> the same concurrent-sweep + recoveryAttempts double-inc window above. The design correctly flags the caveat but understates impact by calling it 'wasted-scan only'.
TTL-vs-claim ordering inside sweepOnce is preserved (read stale -> CAS mark) so the lock does not fix or worsen the existing read-then-CAS window; that part is fine.

DEFINITION semver-hook migration: moving the parse from Schema.pre('save') (definition.model.ts:80-90) to a mongokit before:create/before:update hook changes WHICH write paths populate versionMajor/Minor/Patch. mongokit create routes through _runOp('create') (Repository.ts:556) so the hook fires there, but the current pre('save') ALSO fires on any downstream `doc.save()` / Model.create on the raw exported WorkflowDefinitionModel (index.ts barrel exports it). After migration, a caller still using the raw model's .save() gets NO semver parse and writes a doc missing the required versionMajor fields -> schema validation throw or, worse, silently unsorted versions. This is a behavioral break hidden behind a 'minor-additive' label.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: Respects primitives-not-orchestrator. Confirmed against source: define.ts:148 reads `entry.condition` from the IN-CODE step config on the createWorkflow path; nothing deserializes the DB `condition: String` field (definition.model.ts:30,60) back into runnable code — it is genuinely dead storage. The design's hard boundary ('migrate as inert metadata only, do NOT wire a deserializer that turns the string into runnable code') is the correct and necessary guard and directly upholds the HARD RULE. No business logic, prompts, cost tables, or domain rules move into the engine. One additional caution the design omits: since the migration touches definition.model.ts anyway, the safest philosophy-preserving move is to DROP the `condition: String` field entirely rather than carry it forward — leaving it invites a future contributor to 'just hydrate it'. If kept, it must be documented as forbidden-to-eval.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: Mostly correct and idiomatic, with two misfits. CORRECT: keeping concurrency-counter hand-rolled (incrementIfBelow at mongo-operations.plugin.ts:344-395 confirmed to have NO upsert/$setOnInsert, so it cannot bootstrap a cold counter doc — replacing claimSlot would silently fail to cap a first-call bucket); keeping retention TTL/partial-index DDL hand-rolled (mongokit has no index/TTL builder); leaving per-step embedded steps[] writes on guarded updateOne (claim() keys a single top-level stateField, confirmed Repository.ts CAS shape — it cannot express steps.$.status). MISFIT 1: for the singleton sweep, createMongoLockAdapter (a named global lock, one doc per name) is the right primitive over leasePlugin, agreed — but the design should use the adapter's own defaultLeaseMs semantics and a release() rather than leasing for intervalMs. MISFIT 2: the design proposes WorkflowDefinitionRepository extends Repository + timestampPlugin and 'getLatestVersion/getActiveDefinitions become repo.getAll/aggregate calls' — but getActiveDefinitions uses a $group/$replaceRoot pipeline (definition.model.ts:213-225); mongokit's getAll won't express that, so it stays a raw repo.aggregate() call, which is fine but is NOT the clean win the summary implies. Net: the mongokit usage is a legitimate deepening, not forced, but the definition-repo 'win' is smaller than claimed.
verdict.breakingVerdict: 'minor-additive' is OPTIMISTIC and not fully honest. Three of the four changes are genuinely additive (new WorkflowDefinitionRepository class + deprecated re-export of workflowDefinitionRepository; optional retention.singletonLock config defaulting to current behavior; internal assertWriteConcern test). The HIDDEN break: replacing Schema.pre('save') with a mongokit repo before-hook removes semver-parsing from the raw `WorkflowDefinitionModel` write paths that remain exported from the barrel (index.ts). Any downstream that does `new WorkflowDefinitionModel(...).save()` or `WorkflowDefinitionModel.create()` directly (a documented usage in the file header: 'Load workflows from DB') loses versionMajor/Minor/Patch population — that is a semver-MAJOR-worthy behavior change for direct model users, mislabeled as minor. Fix is to KEEP the pre('save') hook on the schema AND add the repo before-hook (belt-and-suspenders), or stop exporting the raw model. Until then the breaking claim is inaccurate.
requiredFixes: Singleton lock: specify lock.release(name, workerId) in a finally around the sweep, and set leaseMs to a multiple of the MAX expected sweep duration (batchSize * per-row CAS latency), NOT staleRunSweepIntervalMs. Document that intervalMs must be > leaseMs so a holder that finishes does not block the next tick.

Prevent recoveryAttempts double-increment under concurrent sweeps (clock-skew or lease-expiry steal): either make markStaleAsFailed/markAsDeadLettered increment recoveryAttempts via a single atomic $inc inside the same claim() CAS (so the CAS loser cannot also increment), or gate the whole sweep behind the lock with release so concurrency cannot occur. As written, two sweeps can each read attempts=N and both $inc, prematurely dead-lettering. This must be closed before build.

Definition semver-parse: keep Schema.pre('save') in place AND add the mongokit before:create/before:update hook, OR stop exporting the raw WorkflowDefinitionModel from index.ts. Do not silently drop pre('save') while the raw model stays public — that is the hidden break.

Re-label the change set: the definition-model migration is NOT minor-additive for direct raw-model users unless the pre('save') hook is retained. Either retain it (then it is minor) or call out the major-version behavior change explicitly with a migration note.

Drop the `condition: String` field from WorkflowDefinitionDoc/Schema during the migration (preferred), or annotate it FORBIDDEN-TO-EVAL inline, to prevent a future contributor from hydrating dead storage into runnable code and violating the primitives-not-orchestrator rule.

Add the schema-level write-concern regression test (assertWriteConcern on WorkflowRunModel AND concurrency-counter.model) as proposed — this is the only tripwire protecting durability if someone moves write concern off the schema expecting mongokit to carry it per-call (confirmed mongokit has no per-call writeConcern). Keep it.
Document that getActiveDefinitions stays a raw repo.aggregate() ($group/$replaceRoot) call — it cannot be expressed via getAll — so the 'becomes getAll/aggregate' wording is not overclaimed.

design.area: Saga / compensation durability + step-effect idempotency (run lifecycle, executor, status machine, run.model)
design.summary: streamline already exposes a saga surface (`onCompensate` per step, `compensationHandlers`, `workflow:compensating`/`step:compensated` events) but its execution is the one place in the engine that violates the engine's own durability contract: `runCompensation` (engine.ts:727-776) runs AFTER the run is already in terminal `failed`, in a plain in-memory `for` loop, off the state machine, with no per-compensation checkpoint and no recovery path. A crash mid-compensation either loses already-run compensations or re-runs all of them on a manual retry — i.e. double-refund. Separately, idempotency exists only at run-start (whole-workflow dedup via the partial-unique index), never threaded into a step's external side effect. This upgrade hardens the EXISTING saga into a crash-durable, idempotent primitive: a `compensating` run status walked by the state machine, each compensation checkpointed before+after it runs (resume-exact), recovery via the existing stale-sweeper, plus a per-step deterministic `ctx.idempotencyKey` so handlers can make external charge/ship calls effective-once. It is purely additive and adds no business/cost/pricing logic.
design.publicApi:
Additive only. (1) StepConfig gains optional compensation knobs alongside the existing `onCompensate`:
  onCompensate?: StepHandler<unknown, TContext>;                 // unchanged
  compensateRetries?: number;                                    // default 3
  compensateRetryDelay?: number; compensateRetryBackoff?: 'exponential'|'linear'|'fixed'|number;
(2) StepContext gains a deterministic per-(run,step,attempt) idempotency key for effective-once external calls:
  ctx.idempotencyKey(scope?: string): string;   // = `${runId}:${stepId}:${attempt}[:scope]`, stable across crash/resume
  // compensation ctx additionally exposes: ctx.idempotencyKey('compensate') stable across recovery
(3) WorkflowConfig gains an opt-in to durable saga (default keeps today's best-effort behavior for back-compat):
  saga?: { durable?: boolean };   // durable:true => checkpointed, recoverable compensation
(4) New run statuses surfaced in core/status.ts + core/types.ts RunStatus union: 'compensating' | 'compensated' | 'compensation_failed'. deriveRunStatus untouched (these are set explicitly via claim, not derived).
(5) StepState gains a compensation sub-record (new field, NOT the output slot):
  compensation?: { status: 'pending'|'done'|'failed'|'skipped'; attempts: number; startedAt?: Date; completedAt?: Date; error?: StepError };
(6) Two new lifecycle events in EventPayloadMap: 'workflow:compensated', 'workflow:compensation_failed'. Reachable through createWorkflow — no raw-engine use required.
design.internalChanges:
- core/status.ts:107 RUN_MACHINE: add transitions `failed: ['running','compensating']`, `compensating: ['compensated','compensation_failed','compensating']` (self-loop for per-step advance), terminals `compensated`/`compensation_failed`. Without this, assertAndClaim throws before the CAS (per invariant #3).
- core/types.ts:133-150 StepState: add `compensation?` sub-record (new field — honors invariant #9, does NOT reuse `output`). run.model.ts:10-30 schema: mirror the sub-doc; add compound index `{ status:1, 'steps.compensation.status':1 }` for recovery sweeps (run.model.ts:135-256).
- execution/engine.ts:709-776 runCompensation: rewrite from in-memory loop to checkpointed walk. (a) On entry, `assertAndClaim(RUN_MACHINE, failed→compensating)`. (b) Per completed step in reverse: claim `steps.<i>.compensation.status pending→done` only after the handler resolves, written DB-first via buildStepUpdateOps + repo.claim (update-builders.ts:116) with CANCELLED_GUARD + bypassTenant; a step already `compensation.status==='done'` is SKIPPED on replay (memoization = idempotency). (c) Compensation handler honors `compensateRetries` via the same retry/backoff path the executor uses. (d) Terminal claim compensating→compensated (or compensation_failed if any compensation exhausted retries). 
- New recovery seam: storage/run.repository.ts add `getStuckCompensating(threshold)` query (sibling of getStaleRunningWorkflows, run.repository.ts:271-457) + a poll branch in smart-scheduler.ts:449-625 that re-enters runCompensation from the last un-done compensation. recoveryAttempts $inc bound applies (run.model.ts:117) so a wedged compensation dead-letters instead of looping.
- execution/context.ts:29-275 + core/types.ts:295-457: add `idempotencyKey()` — pure derivation from run._id/stepId/attempt already in scope, no write, no new wait kind.
- workflow/define.ts:587-624: thread compensateRetries/backoff into the compensationHandlers registration; `saga.durable` flag onto WorkflowOptions (engine.ts:277 compensationHandlers neighbor).
- index.ts: export new RunStatus literals + event payload types (export type split preserved).
design.mongokitUsage: Leans hard on mongokit's atomic CAS, no new mongokit features required. (1) Each compensation transition uses the inherited `repo.claim(id, {field:'steps.$[i].compensation.status', from:'pending', to:'done', where:{...}}, patch)` shape already used for run transitions (run.repository.ts routes through it so audit/cache/tenant hooks fire) — mongokit Repository.claim (Repository.ts:1477-1650), null-on-miss = another worker won. (2) The `from===to` write-skip fast-path in mongokit claim (Repository.ts:1622-1635) makes idempotent compensation replay cheap — re-claiming an already-'done' compensation short-circuits to findOne, saving a journal flush. (3) Durability is unchanged: claim funnels to Model.findOneAndUpdate, inheriting the schema-level writeConcern {w:'majority',j:true} (run.model.ts:131) — NOT plumbed per-call, exactly as the mongokit map requires. (4) Optionally, where a compensation's effect is itself a write to the same Mongo cluster, the host can wrap business-write + compensation-checkpoint in mongokit `batchTransaction` (transaction.ts:126) for true exactly-once; the engine does not mandate it (external-API compensations stay effective-once via the idempotency key). No leasePlugin/incrementIfBelow needed for this primitive.
design.durabilityModel: Checkpoint-resume (DBOS/Restate model), matching the engine's existing per-step guarantee. Compensation becomes a first-class durable phase: run transitions failed→compensating via assertAndClaim (state-machine-validated, write-first). Each step's compensation is memoized in `steps.<i>.compensation` — written DB-first AFTER the handler resolves, gated by CANCELLED_GUARD. Recovery: if the process crashes mid-compensation, the run sits in `compensating`; the existing StaleRunSweeper (retention.ts) / scheduler poll picks it up via the new getStuckCompensating query and re-enters runCompensation, which SKIPS any step whose compensation.status is already 'done' and resumes from the next — exact-step resume, no double-compensation. Idempotency: (a) compensation memoization gives effective-once compensation within the same cluster; (b) ctx.idempotencyKey() is deterministic across crash/resume so handlers passing it to Stripe/shipping APIs get provider-side effective-once. recoveryAttempts $inc bounds wedged compensations into dead-lettering (markAsDeadLettered claim) rather than infinite loop.
correctnessConcerns: Double-compensation on crash: TODAY's loop re-runs everything on retry — fixed by per-step compensation.status memoization + claim from==='pending'.

Compensation must run AFTER failed is observed but BEFORE the run is considered 'done with failure' by external observers — adding the explicit compensating status closes the window where a watcher sees 'failed' but compensations are still firing.

checkNoProgress (engine.ts:846-865) trap: the compensating self-loop keeps currentStepId constant; the per-compensation claim advancing compensation.status IS the progress signal — must teach checkNoProgress this, exactly as goto marks from-step skipped, or the loop wedges (invariant #7).

Multi-worker race: two workers could both pick up a stuck compensating run — the claim from==='pending'→'done' CAS makes one win, the loser's null-return is treated as 'already done', not error (invariant: null-on-miss).
Reverse-order guarantee must survive resume: recovery must re-derive the reverse-ordered completed-step list deterministically from persisted StepState, not from an in-memory snapshot.
Idempotency key stability: must derive from persisted (runId,stepId,attempt) only — never wall-clock or a counter that resets on resume, or the external call dedup breaks.
Compensation handler that itself waits (ctx.wait) is out of scope — compensation handlers must be non-suspending; validate/throw at define time to avoid a half-parked compensating run.

Cancelled-during-compensation: CANCELLED_GUARD on every compensation write means a cancel mid-compensation stops further compensations — document that cancel does NOT roll back already-run compensations.
design.philosophyNote: Fits, and is bounded to fit. Saga/compensation is a generic durable-execution PRIMITIVE (Temporal/DBOS/Restate all ship it) — not business logic. The engine still holds ZERO domain rules: the host registers `onCompensate` handlers in code (named-handler registry, never serialized executable data); the engine only sequences, checkpoints, and recovers them. No prompt templates, no cost/pricing (cost accounting stays in Prism's pipelines/cost.ts), no order-specific rules enter the engine — the canonical reserve→charge→ship example lives entirely in the HOST's handler functions. The only judgment call is `saga.durable` defaulting to false to preserve v2.3.4's existing best-effort behavior (a published-package back-compat obligation); new code opts into durability explicitly. ctx.idempotencyKey is a pure derivation primitive, not an orchestrator. The one thing to REJECT if it ever creeps in: do NOT add a built-in 'transactional outbox' collection or distributed-lock-per-entity to the engine — that is host/mongokit (leasePlugin, batchTransaction) territory per the mongokit map; the engine should expose the durable checkpoint + idempotency key and let the host compose exactly-once with mongokit transactions.

verdict.area: Saga / compensation durability + step-effect idempotency (engine.runCompensation, executor, RUN_MACHINE/status.ts, run.model StepState, ctx.idempotencyKey)
correctnessHoles: IDEMPOTENCY KEY IS BACKWARDS — keying ctx.idempotencyKey() on `attempt` BREAKS the exact use case it claims to solve. attempt is incremented on every claim (executor.ts:269 `newAttempts = currentStepState.attempts + 1`). A step that calls Stripe, crashes before checkpointing, then retries gets attempt=2 → a DIFFERENT key → provider sees a new request → DOUBLE CHARGE. For effective-once across retries the key MUST be stable across attempts of the same logical step (= `${runId}:${stepId}` only, plus an optional caller scope). Including `attempt` guarantees the double-effect the design exists to prevent. The design even enshrines this mistake in correctnessConcerns ('derive from runId,stepId,attempt').

COMPENSATION CRASH WINDOW IS STILL OPEN at the boundary the design ignores: the checkpoint is written 'DB-first AFTER the handler resolves'. If the host's onCompensate makes an external refund call and the process crashes AFTER the refund succeeds but BEFORE the `pending→done` claim lands, recovery re-runs that compensation (status still 'pending') → double-refund. Memoization only gives effective-once for SAME-CLUSTER writes; for external compensations it provides NOTHING unless the compensation handler itself threads a stable idempotency key — which the broken key above defeats. The 'effective-once compensation' claim is only true for the Mongo-write case, not the canonical Stripe-refund example used to motivate the feature.

runCompensation IS CALLED OUTSIDE THE EXECUTION LOOP (engine.ts:702-711), AFTER cleanupEventListeners + hookRegistry.unregister have already fired and after execute()'s while-loop has broken. The design's model — 'compensating is a status walked by the state machine / re-entered by checkNoProgress self-loop' — does not match this call site. To make compensation a loop-driven phase you must move it INTO the while loop (or build a separate re-entrant executor entrypoint), AND you must reorder cleanup to run only after compensated/compensation_failed is reached, not after `failed`. The design lists the checkNoProgress self-loop trap but never reconciles it with the fact that compensation today runs post-loop, post-cleanup. This is a structural rewrite, not the additive tweak claimed.

MULTI-WORKER COMPENSATION ENTRY RACE: `failed→compensating` via assertAndClaim makes only one worker enter, good. But the existing post-loop call site (engine.ts:709) fires inline on whichever worker observed `failed` — there is no claim there today. Two workers finishing different waiting branches can both reach the terminal block; without the entry-claim being the FIRST thing in the new path, both start compensating before either flips the status. The design asserts the claim but the reverse-order completed-step list is rebuilt from `run.steps` in memory (today's code, line 732) — a stale in-memory snapshot on the losing worker would walk a different/longer list. Recovery must re-read from DB, which the design says, but the inline (non-recovery) entry path does not.

ORDERING UNDER PARTIAL COMPLETION: reverse-order compensation is derived from `s.status === 'done'`. A step that FAILED (the one that triggered the saga abort) is correctly excluded, but a step left in `waiting` (parked on an event/timer when a sibling failed) has a real side effect and no compensation — silent gap. Also steps completed via goto-loops appear once in StepState but may have run their effect multiple times; single compensation under-compensates. Neither is addressed.

STALE-SWEEPER DOUBLE-DRIVE: getStuckCompensating + scheduler poll re-enters runCompensation, but the StaleRunSweeper (retention.ts) keys on lastHeartbeat. Compensation handlers run off the executor's heartbeat timer (the design says compensation reuses 'the same retry/backoff path' but compensation ctx at engine.ts:748 is built WITHOUT abortController/signalStore and is NOT inside executeWithTimeout's heartbeat interval). A long compensation will not heartbeat → the sweeper marks the run stale → markStaleAsFailed races the in-flight compensation. The heartbeat invariant (executor.ts:412/440) is unmet for the compensation phase.
verdict.philosophyVerdict: Philosophy is RESPECTED. Saga/compensation is a genuine durable-execution primitive (Temporal/DBOS/Restate all ship it), not domain logic — handlers stay in host code via the existing named-handler registry (compensationHandlers, define.ts:589-600), nothing is serialized as executable data, no cost/pricing/prompt tables enter the engine. The design explicitly rejects building a transactional-outbox collection or per-entity lock into the engine (correctly punting those to mongokit leasePlugin/batchTransaction in the host). ctx.idempotencyKey is a pure derivation, not an orchestrator. saga.durable defaulting to false to preserve v2.3.4 behavior is a legitimate back-compat call, not a domain leak. No primitives-not-orchestrator violation.
verdict.mongokitVerdict: FORCED / WRONG as written.
